candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
GaussianDreamer: Fast Generation from Text to 3D Gaussians by Bridging
  2D and 3D Diffusion Models
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
In recent times, the generation of 3D assets from text prompts has shown
impressive results. Both 2D and 3D diffusion models can help generate decent 3D
objects based on prompts. 3D diffusion models have good 3D consistency, but
their quality and generalization are limited as trainable 3D data is expensive
and hard to obtain. 2D diffusion models enjoy strong abilities of
generalization and fine generation, but 3D consistency is hard to guarantee.
This paper attempts to bridge the power from the two types of diffusion models
via the recent explicit and efficient 3D Gaussian splatting representation. A
fast 3D object generation framework, named as GaussianDreamer, is proposed,
where the 3D diffusion model provides priors for initialization and the 2D
diffusion model enriches the geometry and appearance. Operations of noisy point
growing and color perturbation are introduced to enhance the initialized
Gaussians. Our GaussianDreamer can generate a high-quality 3D instance or 3D
avatar within 15 minutes on one GPU, much faster than previous methods, while
the generated instances can be directly rendered in real time. Demos and code
are available at https://taoranyi.com/gaussiandreamer/.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents GaussianDreamer, a method that efficiently bridges 3D and 2D diffusion models using Gaussian splatting. It significantly enhances the generation of 3D assets from text prompts, achieving real-time rendering and maintaining high-quality outputs while reducing training time to just 15 minutes on a single GPU.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: GaussianDreamer employs a framework that utilizes both 3D diffusion models for initial Gaussian point generation and 2D diffusion models to enrich the appearance and geometry of 3D instances. It incorporates noisy point growing and color perturbation for initialization, followed by interaction with the 2D model via Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) for optimization.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The method was evaluated using benchmarks like T3Bench, comparing performance metrics against other state-of-the-art methods in text-to-3D generation. The experiments demonstrated the quality of generated 3D assets under varying complexities in prompts, with a focus on both single-object generation and multi-object scenarios.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The generated 3D assets sometimes lack sharp edges and may have unnecessary 3D Gaussians, posing challenges in the filtering process. Additionally, multi-face problems may still occur for objects with minimal geometric differences but significant appearance variations. The method also struggles with generating large-scale scenes.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore improvements in the filtering techniques for 3D Gaussian generation, enhancing the method’s ability to handle complex scenes, and integrating 3D-aware diffusion models to overcome multi-face issues. Expanding the application scope to produce dynamic 3D assets from text prompts could also be a promising avenue.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Source Localization of Graph Diffusion via Variational Autoencoders for
  Graph Inverse Problems
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Graph diffusion problems such as the propagation of rumors, computer viruses,
or smart grid failures are ubiquitous and societal. Hence it is usually crucial
to identify diffusion sources according to the current graph diffusion
observations. Despite its tremendous necessity and significance in practice,
source localization, as the inverse problem of graph diffusion, is extremely
challenging as it is ill-posed: different sources may lead to the same graph
diffusion patterns. Different from most traditional source localization
methods, this paper focuses on a probabilistic manner to account for the
uncertainty of different candidate sources. Such endeavors require overcoming
challenges including 1) the uncertainty in graph diffusion source localization
is hard to be quantified; 2) the complex patterns of the graph diffusion
sources are difficult to be probabilistically characterized; 3) the
generalization under any underlying diffusion patterns is hard to be imposed.
To solve the above challenges, this paper presents a generic framework: Source
Localization Variational AutoEncoder (SL-VAE) for locating the diffusion
sources under arbitrary diffusion patterns. Particularly, we propose a
probabilistic model that leverages the forward diffusion estimation model along
with deep generative models to approximate the diffusion source distribution
for quantifying the uncertainty. SL-VAE further utilizes prior knowledge of the
source-observation pairs to characterize the complex patterns of diffusion
sources by a learned generative prior. Lastly, a unified objective that
integrates the forward diffusion estimation model is derived to enforce the
model to generalize under arbitrary diffusion patterns. Extensive experiments
are conducted on 7 real-world datasets to demonstrate the superiority of SL-VAE
in reconstructing the diffusion sources by excelling other methods on average
20% in AUC score.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper proposes a novel framework called Source Localization Variational AutoEncoder (SL-VAE) for the challenging task of locating the sources of graph diffusion based on observed diffusion patterns. The main contributions include the introduction of a probabilistic model that quantifies uncertainty in source localization, the incorporation of deep generative models to characterize complex patterns of diffusion sources, and a unified objective that generalizes under arbitrary diffusion patterns, achieving superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods on various datasets.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: SL-VAE leverages forward diffusion estimation models combined with deep generative models to approximate the distribution of diffusion sources. Key techniques include variational inference for estimating optimal diffusion sources and conditioning on past observations to encode intrinsic patterns of diffusion behaviors. The model incorporates a novel objective that integrates generative and forward modeling in a unified learning framework.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental setup involves testing SL-VAE on 7 real-world datasets, including Karate, Jazz, Cora-ML, Power Grid, Network Science, Digg, and Memetracker under various diffusion patterns (SI and SIR). Performance is evaluated using metrics such as F1-Score and AUC-ROC curve, and results are compared against several baseline methods including LPSI, GCNSI, OJC, and NetSleuth. Extensive ablation studies and scalability evaluations are also conducted.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: Limitations noted include the complex challenge of quantifying uncertainty due to the ill-posed nature of the problem and the dependence on prior knowledge which could impact performance if not accurately characterized. There are also potential computational inefficiencies in marginalizing over latent variables and the need for careful design of forward models to adapt to varying graph structures.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore further optimizations of the SL-VAE framework, improvements in computational efficiency, and adaptations to a wider variety of graph types and diffusion patterns. Additionally, integrating advanced deep learning techniques or hybrid models could enhance the robustness and flexibility of the approach.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Carve3D: Improving Multi-view Reconstruction Consistency for Diffusion
  Models with RL Finetuning
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Multi-view diffusion models, obtained by applying Supervised Finetuning (SFT)
to text-to-image diffusion models, have driven recent breakthroughs in
text-to-3D research. However, due to the limited size and quality of existing
3D datasets, they still suffer from multi-view inconsistencies and Neural
Radiance Field (NeRF) reconstruction artifacts. We argue that multi-view
diffusion models can benefit from further Reinforcement Learning Finetuning
(RLFT), which allows models to learn from the data generated by themselves and
improve beyond their dataset limitations during SFT. To this end, we introduce
Carve3D, an improved RLFT algorithm coupled with a novel Multi-view
Reconstruction Consistency (MRC) metric, to enhance the consistency of
multi-view diffusion models. To measure the MRC metric on a set of multi-view
images, we compare them with their corresponding NeRF renderings at the same
camera viewpoints. The resulting model, which we denote as Carve3DM,
demonstrates superior multi-view consistency and NeRF reconstruction quality
than existing models. Our results suggest that pairing SFT with Carve3D's RLFT
is essential for developing multi-view-consistent diffusion models, mirroring
the standard Large Language Model (LLM) alignment pipeline. Our code, training
and testing data, and video results are available at:
https://desaixie.github.io/carve-3d.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces Carve3D, an improved Reinforcement Learning Finetuning (RLFT) algorithm that enhances the consistency of multi-view diffusion models by addressing multi-view reconstruction inconsistencies and neural artifacts, along with introducing a novel Multi-view Reconstruction Consistency (MRC) metric for evaluation.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology includes the application of RLFT to optimize multi-view diffusion models beyond dataset limitations through self-generated data. The MRC metric compares generated multi-view images to NeRF renderings at identical viewpoints, using a policy gradient approach for training.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments utilized the Instant3D-10K model and involved quantitative comparisons with Instant3D models at different SFT steps (10K, 20K, 100K) and other baseline models like MVDream and SyncDreamer. Data was generated from the DreamFusion prompt set, assessing MRC during training.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The reconstruction quality heavily depends on the Sparse View Large Reconstruction Model (LRM), which can introduce artifacts. The study is also constrained by the computation costs associated with high-resolution models and limited dataset sizes for effective fine-tuning.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work may include improving the reconstruction quality of sparse view models, exploring alternative RLFT approaches, and scaling the method to larger datasets or different model architectures to enhance model performance and consistency.

candidate_base_papers_info_list.title:
Masked-Attention Diffusion Guidance for Spatially Controlling
  Text-to-Image Generation
candidate_base_papers_info_list.summary:
Text-to-image synthesis has achieved high-quality results with recent
advances in diffusion models. However, text input alone has high spatial
ambiguity and limited user controllability. Most existing methods allow spatial
control through additional visual guidance (e.g., sketches and semantic masks)
but require additional training with annotated images. In this paper, we
propose a method for spatially controlling text-to-image generation without
further training of diffusion models. Our method is based on the insight that
the cross-attention maps reflect the positional relationship between words and
pixels. Our aim is to control the attention maps according to given semantic
masks and text prompts. To this end, we first explore a simple approach of
directly swapping the cross-attention maps with constant maps computed from the
semantic regions. Some prior works also allow training-free spatial control of
text-to-image diffusion models by directly manipulating cross-attention maps.
However, these approaches still suffer from misalignment to given masks because
manipulated attention maps are far from actual ones learned by diffusion
models. To address this issue, we propose masked-attention guidance, which can
generate images more faithful to semantic masks via indirect control of
attention to each word and pixel by manipulating noise images fed to diffusion
models. Masked-attention guidance can be easily integrated into pre-trained
off-the-shelf diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) and applied to the
tasks of text-guided image editing. Experiments show that our method enables
more accurate spatial control than baselines qualitatively and quantitatively.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research addresses the problem of spatial control in text-to-image generation without the need for additional training of diffusion models. The proposed method, called masked-attention guidance, allows for more faithful image generation aligned with semantic masks and text prompts compared to existing training-free methods like MultiDiffusion. The method emphasizes controlling attention maps derived from cross-attention layers during image generation.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.methodology: The primary techniques used include masked-attention swapping and masked-attention guidance. The latter involves manipulating noise maps fed to diffusion models to adjust the cross-attention maps indirectly based on semantic masks and corresponding text prompts, leveraging the inherent capabilities of well-trained diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion).
candidate_base_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments utilize the COCO dataset, focusing on images with annotated instance masks. The main evaluation metrics are mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) against ground-truth segmentation and FIDCLIP scores for image quality. The method runs on Stable Diffusion v2.0 with 50 sampling steps using a DDIM sampler, filtering images to those with 2-4 instances for generating text-to-image outputs.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.limitations: The method struggles with generating images perfectly aligned with given masks, especially for small regions and multiple instances with identical words. Additionally, using a strong guidance scale can lead to unnatural image outputs. The integration into other diffusion models could allow for finer control, but current limitations exist due to inefficiencies in processing low-dimensional latent spaces.
candidate_base_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could involve extending the method to handle other forms of visual guidance, such as sparse scribbles, and improving the coordination of multiple instance masks corresponding to the same object, potentially enhancing the quality of generated images.

selected_base_paper_info.title:
GaussianDreamer: Fast Generation from Text to 3D Gaussians by Bridging
  2D and 3D Diffusion Models
selected_base_paper_info.summary:
In recent times, the generation of 3D assets from text prompts has shown
impressive results. Both 2D and 3D diffusion models can help generate decent 3D
objects based on prompts. 3D diffusion models have good 3D consistency, but
their quality and generalization are limited as trainable 3D data is expensive
and hard to obtain. 2D diffusion models enjoy strong abilities of
generalization and fine generation, but 3D consistency is hard to guarantee.
This paper attempts to bridge the power from the two types of diffusion models
via the recent explicit and efficient 3D Gaussian splatting representation. A
fast 3D object generation framework, named as GaussianDreamer, is proposed,
where the 3D diffusion model provides priors for initialization and the 2D
diffusion model enriches the geometry and appearance. Operations of noisy point
growing and color perturbation are introduced to enhance the initialized
Gaussians. Our GaussianDreamer can generate a high-quality 3D instance or 3D
avatar within 15 minutes on one GPU, much faster than previous methods, while
the generated instances can be directly rendered in real time. Demos and code
are available at https://taoranyi.com/gaussiandreamer/.
selected_base_paper_info.main_contributions: The paper presents GaussianDreamer, a method that efficiently bridges 3D and 2D diffusion models using Gaussian splatting. It significantly enhances the generation of 3D assets from text prompts, achieving real-time rendering and maintaining high-quality outputs while reducing training time to just 15 minutes on a single GPU.
selected_base_paper_info.methodology: GaussianDreamer employs a framework that utilizes both 3D diffusion models for initial Gaussian point generation and 2D diffusion models to enrich the appearance and geometry of 3D instances. It incorporates noisy point growing and color perturbation for initialization, followed by interaction with the 2D model via Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) for optimization.
selected_base_paper_info.experimental_setup: The method was evaluated using benchmarks like T3Bench, comparing performance metrics against other state-of-the-art methods in text-to-3D generation. The experiments demonstrated the quality of generated 3D assets under varying complexities in prompts, with a focus on both single-object generation and multi-object scenarios.
selected_base_paper_info.limitations: The generated 3D assets sometimes lack sharp edges and may have unnecessary 3D Gaussians, posing challenges in the filtering process. Additionally, multi-face problems may still occur for objects with minimal geometric differences but significant appearance variations. The method also struggles with generating large-scale scenes.
selected_base_paper_info.future_research_directions: Future work could explore improvements in the filtering techniques for 3D Gaussian generation, enhancing the method’s ability to handle complex scenes, and integrating 3D-aware diffusion models to overcome multi-face issues. Expanding the application scope to produce dynamic 3D assets from text prompts could also be a promising avenue.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Advancing ALS Applications with Large-Scale Pre-training: Dataset
  Development and Downstream Assessment
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
The pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm has revolutionized satellite remote
sensing applications. However, this approach remains largely underexplored for
airborne laser scanning (ALS), an important technology for applications such as
forest management and urban planning. In this study, we address this gap by
constructing a large-scale ALS point cloud dataset and evaluating its impact on
downstream applications. Our dataset comprises ALS point clouds collected
across the contiguous United States, provided by the United States Geological
Survey's 3D Elevation Program. To ensure efficient data collection while
capturing diverse land cover and terrain types, we introduce a geospatial
sampling method that selects point cloud tiles based on land cover maps and
digital elevation models. As a baseline self-supervised learning model, we
adopt BEV-MAE, a state-of-the-art masked autoencoder for 3D outdoor point
clouds, and pre-train it on the constructed dataset. The pre-trained models are
subsequently fine-tuned for downstream tasks, including tree species
classification, terrain scene recognition, and point cloud semantic
segmentation. Our results show that the pre-trained models significantly
outperform their scratch counterparts across all downstream tasks,
demonstrating the transferability of the representations learned from the
proposed dataset. Furthermore, we observe that scaling the dataset using our
geospatial sampling method consistently enhances performance, whereas
pre-training on datasets constructed with random sampling fails to achieve
similar improvements. These findings highlight the utility of the constructed
dataset and the effectiveness of our sampling strategy in the pre-training and
fine-tuning paradigm. The source code and pre-trained models will be made
publicly available at \url{https://github.com/martianxiu/ALS_pretraining}.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This research addresses the gap in applying large-scale pre-training to Airborne Laser Scanning (ALS) applications by constructing a large-scale ALS point cloud dataset and demonstrating its effectiveness in downstream tasks such as tree species classification, terrain scene recognition, and point cloud semantic segmentation. The study introduces a geospatial sampling method based on land cover maps and digital elevation models to ensure diverse and representative data collection, resulting in significant improvements in model performance compared to untrained models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The study utilizes BEV-MAE, a state-of-the-art masked autoencoder model designed for 3D outdoor point clouds, for pre-training on the constructed dataset. The geospatial sampling method developed for data collection is a key innovation, enabling efficient extraction of diverse ALS point cloud data to enhance model generalization capabilities across various tasks.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The dataset comprises ALS point clouds from the USGS 3D Elevation Program, covering diverse terrains and land cover across the contiguous United States. The model is pre-trained and fine-tuned for three downstream tasks: tree species classification using the PureForest dataset, terrain scene recognition based on OpenGF, and point cloud semantic segmentation utilizing the DALES dataset. Various metrics, including mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) and overall accuracy (OA), are employed to evaluate performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The model shows limited performance improvement in point cloud semantic segmentation tasks, potentially due to its inability to capture fine-grained object details in developed areas. Additionally, existing sampling methods and dataset scaling may not fully address the specific challenges posed by variations in object scales within ALS data.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could involve refining the pre-training framework with enhancements such as perceptual loss for better geometric reconstruction, the creation of tailored self-supervised learning methods specifically for ALS data, and the exploration of alternative masking strategies to better handle both large and small-scale objects.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: DiffAssemble: A Unified Graph-Diffusion Model for 2D and 3D Reassembly
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Reassembly tasks play a fundamental role in many fields and multiple
approaches exist to solve specific reassembly problems. In this context, we
posit that a general unified model can effectively address them all,
irrespective of the input data type (images, 3D, etc.). We introduce
DiffAssemble, a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based architecture that learns to
solve reassembly tasks using a diffusion model formulation. Our method treats
the elements of a set, whether pieces of 2D patch or 3D object fragments, as
nodes of a spatial graph. Training is performed by introducing noise into the
position and rotation of the elements and iteratively denoising them to
reconstruct the coherent initial pose. DiffAssemble achieves state-of-the-art
(SOTA) results in most 2D and 3D reassembly tasks and is the first
learning-based approach that solves 2D puzzles for both rotation and
translation. Furthermore, we highlight its remarkable reduction in run-time,
performing 11 times faster than the quickest optimization-based method for
puzzle solving. Code available at https://github.com/IIT-PAVIS/DiffAssemble
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: DiffAssemble introduces a unified Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based architecture designed for reassembly tasks in both 2D and 3D environments, achieving state-of-the-art results in these domains. It can handle 2D puzzles that involve both rotation and translation while significantly improving runtime performance compared to optimization-based methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology leverages a diffusion model formulation within a graph framework, where the pieces to be reassembled are represented as nodes in a spatial graph. DiffAssemble uses an iterative denoising process, starting with noise added to the positions and orientations of the pieces, guided by an Attention-based GNN to recover the original poses.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental setup involves two main datasets: Breaking Bad for 3D object reassembly and PuzzleCelebA and PuzzleWikiArts for 2D jigsaw puzzles. The paper evaluates the model using metrics such as Root Mean Squared Error for rotation and translation, and Part Accuracy for 3D tasks. It compares DiffAssemble against several benchmarks, detailing its improved accuracy and runtime efficiency via various ablation studies.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: A major limitation of DiffAssemble is its high memory usage despite employing a sparsity mechanism to manage larger graphs, which may restrict its applicability in resource-constrained environments.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on reducing memory demands, improving the efficiency of the model for larger instances, and exploring additional reassembly scenarios utilizing real-world scan data.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: DiffEdit: Diffusion-based semantic image editing with mask guidance
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Image generation has recently seen tremendous advances, with diffusion models
allowing to synthesize convincing images for a large variety of text prompts.
In this article, we propose DiffEdit, a method to take advantage of
text-conditioned diffusion models for the task of semantic image editing, where
the goal is to edit an image based on a text query. Semantic image editing is
an extension of image generation, with the additional constraint that the
generated image should be as similar as possible to a given input image.
Current editing methods based on diffusion models usually require to provide a
mask, making the task much easier by treating it as a conditional inpainting
task. In contrast, our main contribution is able to automatically generate a
mask highlighting regions of the input image that need to be edited, by
contrasting predictions of a diffusion model conditioned on different text
prompts. Moreover, we rely on latent inference to preserve content in those
regions of interest and show excellent synergies with mask-based diffusion.
DiffEdit achieves state-of-the-art editing performance on ImageNet. In
addition, we evaluate semantic image editing in more challenging settings,
using images from the COCO dataset as well as text-based generated images.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces DIFFEDIT, a novel semantic image editing algorithm that utilizes text-conditioned diffusion models. It highlights the ability of DIFFEDIT to automatically generate masks indicating which areas of the input image should be edited, achieving state-of-the-art editing performance on datasets like ImageNet and COCO while preserving actionable content in unedited regions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: DIFFEDIT employs a three-step approach: (1) Compute an editing mask by contrasting noise predictions from the diffusion model using different text conditions, (2) Encode the input image using a DDIM (Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models) approach to preserve input information, and (3) Decode with guidance from the mask to ensure edits are localized and do not alter the background.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments are conducted on three datasets: ImageNet, generated images from the Imagen framework, and the COCO dataset. The editing performance is evaluated using various metrics such as CSFID (class-conditional FID) and LPIPS (Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity) to measure the realism and fidelity of the edits. The diffusion model used is latent diffusion, specifically trained on the aforementioned datasets.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The paper notes the reliance on high-quality text prompts for effective mask generation, which can limit the algorithm's robustness in casual usage. It also acknowledges the existing biases in datasets upon which the models were trained, which may affect the outputs, especially in culturally diverse contexts.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Potential avenues for future research include improving mask generation techniques to reduce dependency on accurate textual descriptions, exploring the integration of DIFFEDIT with more complex generative models, and addressing ethical concerns related to biases inherent in the training datasets.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Fast ODE-based Sampling for Diffusion Models in Around 5 Steps
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Sampling from diffusion models can be treated as solving the corresponding
ordinary differential equations (ODEs), with the aim of obtaining an accurate
solution with as few number of function evaluations (NFE) as possible.
Recently, various fast samplers utilizing higher-order ODE solvers have emerged
and achieved better performance than the initial first-order one. However,
these numerical methods inherently result in certain approximation errors,
which significantly degrades sample quality with extremely small NFE (e.g.,
around 5). In contrast, based on the geometric observation that each sampling
trajectory almost lies in a two-dimensional subspace embedded in the ambient
space, we propose Approximate MEan-Direction Solver (AMED-Solver) that
eliminates truncation errors by directly learning the mean direction for fast
diffusion sampling. Besides, our method can be easily used as a plugin to
further improve existing ODE-based samplers. Extensive experiments on image
synthesis with the resolution ranging from 32 to 512 demonstrate the
effectiveness of our method. With only 5 NFE, we achieve 6.61 FID on CIFAR-10,
10.74 FID on ImageNet 64$\times$64, and 13.20 FID on LSUN Bedroom. Our code is
available at https://github.com/zju-pi/diff-sampler.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper proposes the Approximate MEan-Direction Solver (AMED-Solver), a new single-step ODE solver for diffusion models aimed at minimizing discretization errors in sampling. It shows significant improvements in sample quality with only around 5 function evaluations (NFE), achieving state-of-the-art Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) scores on various datasets.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The AMED-Solver learns to predict mean directions for fast diffusion sampling, leveraging the geometric observation that sampling trajectories lie in a two-dimensional subspace. Additionally, an improvement plugin (AMED-Plugin) is introduced to enhance existing ODE solvers.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted on datasets like CIFAR-10, ImageNet, and LSUN Bedroom, evaluating image synthesis quality at different resolutions (32 to 512). The sampling quality was assessed using FID scores calculated with 50,000 images generated during testing, and several baseline ODE solvers were compared.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The methodology is sensitive to the choice of time schedules, which can affect performance significantly. Fixed schedules may not work optimally across all scenarios, indicating a need for better design in future use.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could focus on exploring adaptive time scheduling methods and further refining the geometric understanding of sampling trajectories in diffusion models.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: SVGDreamer: Text Guided SVG Generation with Diffusion Model
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Recently, text-guided scalable vector graphics (SVGs) synthesis has shown
promise in domains such as iconography and sketch. However, existing
text-to-SVG generation methods lack editability and struggle with visual
quality and result diversity. To address these limitations, we propose a novel
text-guided vector graphics synthesis method called SVGDreamer. SVGDreamer
incorporates a semantic-driven image vectorization (SIVE) process that enables
the decomposition of synthesis into foreground objects and background, thereby
enhancing editability. Specifically, the SIVE process introduces
attention-based primitive control and an attention-mask loss function for
effective control and manipulation of individual elements. Additionally, we
propose a Vectorized Particle-based Score Distillation (VPSD) approach to
address issues of shape over-smoothing, color over-saturation, limited
diversity, and slow convergence of the existing text-to-SVG generation methods
by modeling SVGs as distributions of control points and colors. Furthermore,
VPSD leverages a reward model to re-weight vector particles, which improves
aesthetic appeal and accelerates convergence. Extensive experiments are
conducted to validate the effectiveness of SVGDreamer, demonstrating its
superiority over baseline methods in terms of editability, visual quality, and
diversity. Project page: https://ximinng.github.io/SVGDreamer-project/
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper presents SVGDreamer, a method for text-guided SVG generation that enhances visual quality, editability, and diversity of generated graphics. It introduces a Semantic-driven Image Vectorization (SIVE) process and a Vectorized Particle-based Score Distillation (VPSD) approach to address limitations in existing text-to-SVG generation methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: SVGDreamer employs a two-part approach where SIVE is used for semantic-driven control point initialization and object separation, utilizing attention maps from a diffusion model. VPSD is employed to refine the generated SVGs by modeling them as distributions of control points and colors, and incorporates a reward model for optimizing aesthetic appeal and convergence.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experiments involved comprehensive qualitative and quantitative evaluations comparing SVGDreamer against baseline methods like CLIPDraw and VectorFusion, using metrics such as Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), CLIPScore, and aesthetic scores. Various styles were tested across multiple prompts.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The editability of SVGDreamer heavily relies on the text-to-image model used, which could limit its performance. Future advancements in T2I models may improve its capabilities. Additionally, automatic determination of control points at the SIVE level remains an unexplored area.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could focus on improving the editability by developing more sophisticated T2I models, exploring automatic control point determination, and further refining the SIVE and VPSD methods for better integration and diversity in outputs.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
Carve3D: Improving Multi-view Reconstruction Consistency for Diffusion
  Models with RL Finetuning
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Multi-view diffusion models, obtained by applying Supervised Finetuning (SFT)
to text-to-image diffusion models, have driven recent breakthroughs in
text-to-3D research. However, due to the limited size and quality of existing
3D datasets, they still suffer from multi-view inconsistencies and Neural
Radiance Field (NeRF) reconstruction artifacts. We argue that multi-view
diffusion models can benefit from further Reinforcement Learning Finetuning
(RLFT), which allows models to learn from the data generated by themselves and
improve beyond their dataset limitations during SFT. To this end, we introduce
Carve3D, an improved RLFT algorithm coupled with a novel Multi-view
Reconstruction Consistency (MRC) metric, to enhance the consistency of
multi-view diffusion models. To measure the MRC metric on a set of multi-view
images, we compare them with their corresponding NeRF renderings at the same
camera viewpoints. The resulting model, which we denote as Carve3DM,
demonstrates superior multi-view consistency and NeRF reconstruction quality
than existing models. Our results suggest that pairing SFT with Carve3D's RLFT
is essential for developing multi-view-consistent diffusion models, mirroring
the standard Large Language Model (LLM) alignment pipeline. Our code, training
and testing data, and video results are available at:
https://desaixie.github.io/carve-3d.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research presents Carve3D, a new reinforcement learning finetuning (RLFT) algorithm aimed at improving multi-view consistency in diffusion models, addressing issues of inconsistency and artifacts in existing models caused by limited 3D datasets. The introduction of a Multi-view Reconstruction Consistency (MRC) metric facilitates evaluation and enhancement of multi-view image consistency, demonstrating Carve3DM improves upon previous models both quantitatively and qualitatively.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: Carve3D employs an RLFT approach, using a policy gradient algorithm to finetune a pre-trained multi-view diffusion model based on generated images and their MRC rewards. The method leverages the Large Reconstruction Model (LRM) for rapid NeRF reconstruction and applies the perceptual LPIPS metric for evaluating image consistency. It incorporates KL divergence regularization to mitigate distribution shifts during finetuning.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The study utilizes the Instant3D-10K dataset as a base multi-view diffusion model, employing a custom set of curated text prompts for training, each producing multiple diverse outputs to evaluate MRC. Experiments consist of quantitative assessments comparing Carve3DM against models like Instant3D with varying degrees of supervised fine-tuning steps (10K, 20K, 100K) and qualitative user studies measuring 3D consistency and prompt alignment on select prompts.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The Carve3D approach is limited by the quality and fidelity of the Sparse View Large Reconstruction Model used for NeRF, which may negatively impact high-frequency details in generated outputs. Additionally, the scalability of the method is constrained by the computational demands during training and the size of the 3D datasets, which restricts broader applicability to diverse models.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work may explore extending the MRC metric to other generative models, improving the framework's efficiency through alternative algorithms or reconstruction techniques such as Consistency Models, and further optimizing data preparation and scaling strategies to enhance training capability without degrading output quality.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: Shadow Generation for Composite Image Using Diffusion model
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
In the realm of image composition, generating realistic shadow for the
inserted foreground remains a formidable challenge. Previous works have
developed image-to-image translation models which are trained on paired
training data. However, they are struggling to generate shadows with accurate
shapes and intensities, hindered by data scarcity and inherent task complexity.
In this paper, we resort to foundation model with rich prior knowledge of
natural shadow images. Specifically, we first adapt ControlNet to our task and
then propose intensity modulation modules to improve the shadow intensity.
Moreover, we extend the small-scale DESOBA dataset to DESOBAv2 using a novel
data acquisition pipeline. Experimental results on both DESOBA and DESOBAv2
datasets as well as real composite images demonstrate the superior capability
of our model for shadow generation task. The dataset, code, and model are
released at https://github.com/bcmi/Object-Shadow-Generation-Dataset-DESOBAv2.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The research addresses the challenge of generating realistic shadows for inserted foreground objects in composite images. It introduces a novel shadow generation method using a diffusion model, adapted from ControlNet, and presents a large-scale shadow generation dataset, DESOBAv2, which significantly enhances the number of real image pairs for training.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves adapting ControlNet for shadow generation by modifying its control encoder input and integrating intensity modulation modules to enhance shadow intensity. The proposed SGDiffusion model also uses a post-processing network to rectify color shifts and background variations.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using two datasets: the original DESOBA dataset and a larger DESOBAv2 dataset with 21,575 real images and 28,573 pairs. Evaluation metrics included RMSE and SSIM for image quality, and Balanced Error Rate (BER) for mask accuracy. The approach compared results against baseline methods for both quantitative and qualitative analysis.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The research does not mention specific limitations in terms of model applicability to all scenarios or the potential biases introduced by the training datasets. However, some issues with generating shadows for complex or transparent objects were noted, indicating challenges in generalization in certain cases.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore enhancements in shadow generation for more complex scenes, better handling of transparency and occlusion in shadows, and the development of additional datasets to improve model robustness and address the varied lighting conditions.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title: SVGDreamer: Text Guided SVG Generation with Diffusion Model
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Recently, text-guided scalable vector graphics (SVGs) synthesis has shown
promise in domains such as iconography and sketch. However, existing
text-to-SVG generation methods lack editability and struggle with visual
quality and result diversity. To address these limitations, we propose a novel
text-guided vector graphics synthesis method called SVGDreamer. SVGDreamer
incorporates a semantic-driven image vectorization (SIVE) process that enables
the decomposition of synthesis into foreground objects and background, thereby
enhancing editability. Specifically, the SIVE process introduces
attention-based primitive control and an attention-mask loss function for
effective control and manipulation of individual elements. Additionally, we
propose a Vectorized Particle-based Score Distillation (VPSD) approach to
address issues of shape over-smoothing, color over-saturation, limited
diversity, and slow convergence of the existing text-to-SVG generation methods
by modeling SVGs as distributions of control points and colors. Furthermore,
VPSD leverages a reward model to re-weight vector particles, which improves
aesthetic appeal and accelerates convergence. Extensive experiments are
conducted to validate the effectiveness of SVGDreamer, demonstrating its
superiority over baseline methods in terms of editability, visual quality, and
diversity. Project page: https://ximinng.github.io/SVGDreamer-project/
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces SVGDreamer, a novel model for text-guided SVG generation that enhances editability and visual quality while addressing limitations in existing text-to-SVG methods, including over-smoothing and lack of diversity.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: The SVGDreamer method consists of two key components: Semantic-Driven Image Vectorization (SIVE) for decomposing SVG generation into foreground and background via attention-based control, and Vectorized Particle-based Score Distillation (VPSD) for refining vector graphics by modeling SVGs as distributions of control points and colors.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: Extensive experiments were conducted using various datasets covering different SVG styles including iconography and sketches. Performance was evaluated based on metrics such as FID, PSNR, CLIPScore, BLIPScore, Aesthetic Score, and Human Performance Score across multiple prompts and synthesized SVGs.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: The editability of SVGDreamer is currently constrained by the abilities of the underlying text-to-image model. Additionally, improvements in determining the optimal number of control points for different objects would be beneficial.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work may focus on enhancing text-to-image diffusion models to improve decomposition capabilities, automating control point determination, and exploring additional styles and applications for SVGDreamer.

candidate_add_papers_info_list.title:
NoiseCollage: A Layout-Aware Text-to-Image Diffusion Model Based on
  Noise Cropping and Merging
candidate_add_papers_info_list.summary:
Layout-aware text-to-image generation is a task to generate multi-object
images that reflect layout conditions in addition to text conditions. The
current layout-aware text-to-image diffusion models still have several issues,
including mismatches between the text and layout conditions and quality
degradation of generated images. This paper proposes a novel layout-aware
text-to-image diffusion model called NoiseCollage to tackle these issues.
During the denoising process, NoiseCollage independently estimates noises for
individual objects and then crops and merges them into a single noise. This
operation helps avoid condition mismatches; in other words, it can put the
right objects in the right places. Qualitative and quantitative evaluations
show that NoiseCollage outperforms several state-of-the-art models. These
successful results indicate that the crop-and-merge operation of noises is a
reasonable strategy to control image generation. We also show that NoiseCollage
can be integrated with ControlNet to use edges, sketches, and pose skeletons as
additional conditions. Experimental results show that this integration boosts
the layout accuracy of ControlNet. The code is available at
https://github.com/univ-esuty/noisecollage.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.main_contributions: This paper introduces NoiseCollage, a novel layout-aware text-to-image diffusion model that independently estimates noises for individual objects and merges them to improve the accuracy of generated images in aligning with both text and layout conditions. It demonstrates that this crop-and-merge operation effectively minimizes mismatches and enhances image quality compared to state-of-the-art methods.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.methodology: NoiseCollage employs a denoising process where it estimates N+1 noise vectors independently for N individual objects and a global scene. It uses a soft crop-and-merge operation to combine these noise estimates and implements a masked cross-attention mechanism to ensure that the features of individual objects are localized correctly around predefined layout regions.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental evaluation utilizes two datasets: BD807, consisting of 807 images from the MS-COCO dataset, and MD30, a smaller dataset with 30 manually annotated images. Evaluations were performed by calculating multimodal feature similarities between generated images and their corresponding text conditions using CLIP. The model was compared against other layout-aware diffusion models to assess performance.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.limitations: NoiseCollage can overlook small objects in generated images, and while it performs better than other models in many aspects, it still encounters difficulties in generating images with complex layouts involving many small items. Furthermore, it assumes that layout conditions are given explicitly as bounding boxes or polygons.
candidate_add_papers_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could explore automatic inference of layout conditions from text descriptions, as well as extending the model to accept point annotations for object locations. Another direction could involve investigating the relationships between noise representation and various transformations to enable the generation of multi-object videos.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title: DiffAssemble: A Unified Graph-Diffusion Model for 2D and 3D Reassembly
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Reassembly tasks play a fundamental role in many fields and multiple
approaches exist to solve specific reassembly problems. In this context, we
posit that a general unified model can effectively address them all,
irrespective of the input data type (images, 3D, etc.). We introduce
DiffAssemble, a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based architecture that learns to
solve reassembly tasks using a diffusion model formulation. Our method treats
the elements of a set, whether pieces of 2D patch or 3D object fragments, as
nodes of a spatial graph. Training is performed by introducing noise into the
position and rotation of the elements and iteratively denoising them to
reconstruct the coherent initial pose. DiffAssemble achieves state-of-the-art
(SOTA) results in most 2D and 3D reassembly tasks and is the first
learning-based approach that solves 2D puzzles for both rotation and
translation. Furthermore, we highlight its remarkable reduction in run-time,
performing 11 times faster than the quickest optimization-based method for
puzzle solving. Code available at https://github.com/IIT-PAVIS/DiffAssemble
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: DiffAssemble introduces a unified Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based architecture designed for reassembly tasks in both 2D and 3D environments, achieving state-of-the-art results in these domains. It can handle 2D puzzles that involve both rotation and translation while significantly improving runtime performance compared to optimization-based methods.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The methodology leverages a diffusion model formulation within a graph framework, where the pieces to be reassembled are represented as nodes in a spatial graph. DiffAssemble uses an iterative denoising process, starting with noise added to the positions and orientations of the pieces, guided by an Attention-based GNN to recover the original poses.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The experimental setup involves two main datasets: Breaking Bad for 3D object reassembly and PuzzleCelebA and PuzzleWikiArts for 2D jigsaw puzzles. The paper evaluates the model using metrics such as Root Mean Squared Error for rotation and translation, and Part Accuracy for 3D tasks. It compares DiffAssemble against several benchmarks, detailing its improved accuracy and runtime efficiency via various ablation studies.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: A major limitation of DiffAssemble is its high memory usage despite employing a sparsity mechanism to manage larger graphs, which may restrict its applicability in resource-constrained environments.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could focus on reducing memory demands, improving the efficiency of the model for larger instances, and exploring additional reassembly scenarios utilizing real-world scan data.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title: DiffEdit: Diffusion-based semantic image editing with mask guidance
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Image generation has recently seen tremendous advances, with diffusion models
allowing to synthesize convincing images for a large variety of text prompts.
In this article, we propose DiffEdit, a method to take advantage of
text-conditioned diffusion models for the task of semantic image editing, where
the goal is to edit an image based on a text query. Semantic image editing is
an extension of image generation, with the additional constraint that the
generated image should be as similar as possible to a given input image.
Current editing methods based on diffusion models usually require to provide a
mask, making the task much easier by treating it as a conditional inpainting
task. In contrast, our main contribution is able to automatically generate a
mask highlighting regions of the input image that need to be edited, by
contrasting predictions of a diffusion model conditioned on different text
prompts. Moreover, we rely on latent inference to preserve content in those
regions of interest and show excellent synergies with mask-based diffusion.
DiffEdit achieves state-of-the-art editing performance on ImageNet. In
addition, we evaluate semantic image editing in more challenging settings,
using images from the COCO dataset as well as text-based generated images.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The paper introduces DIFFEDIT, a novel semantic image editing algorithm that utilizes text-conditioned diffusion models. It highlights the ability of DIFFEDIT to automatically generate masks indicating which areas of the input image should be edited, achieving state-of-the-art editing performance on datasets like ImageNet and COCO while preserving actionable content in unedited regions.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: DIFFEDIT employs a three-step approach: (1) Compute an editing mask by contrasting noise predictions from the diffusion model using different text conditions, (2) Encode the input image using a DDIM (Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models) approach to preserve input information, and (3) Decode with guidance from the mask to ensure edits are localized and do not alter the background.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments are conducted on three datasets: ImageNet, generated images from the Imagen framework, and the COCO dataset. The editing performance is evaluated using various metrics such as CSFID (class-conditional FID) and LPIPS (Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity) to measure the realism and fidelity of the edits. The diffusion model used is latent diffusion, specifically trained on the aforementioned datasets.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The paper notes the reliance on high-quality text prompts for effective mask generation, which can limit the algorithm's robustness in casual usage. It also acknowledges the existing biases in datasets upon which the models were trained, which may affect the outputs, especially in culturally diverse contexts.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Potential avenues for future research include improving mask generation techniques to reduce dependency on accurate textual descriptions, exploring the integration of DIFFEDIT with more complex generative models, and addressing ethical concerns related to biases inherent in the training datasets.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title: Fast ODE-based Sampling for Diffusion Models in Around 5 Steps
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Sampling from diffusion models can be treated as solving the corresponding
ordinary differential equations (ODEs), with the aim of obtaining an accurate
solution with as few number of function evaluations (NFE) as possible.
Recently, various fast samplers utilizing higher-order ODE solvers have emerged
and achieved better performance than the initial first-order one. However,
these numerical methods inherently result in certain approximation errors,
which significantly degrades sample quality with extremely small NFE (e.g.,
around 5). In contrast, based on the geometric observation that each sampling
trajectory almost lies in a two-dimensional subspace embedded in the ambient
space, we propose Approximate MEan-Direction Solver (AMED-Solver) that
eliminates truncation errors by directly learning the mean direction for fast
diffusion sampling. Besides, our method can be easily used as a plugin to
further improve existing ODE-based samplers. Extensive experiments on image
synthesis with the resolution ranging from 32 to 512 demonstrate the
effectiveness of our method. With only 5 NFE, we achieve 6.61 FID on CIFAR-10,
10.74 FID on ImageNet 64$\times$64, and 13.20 FID on LSUN Bedroom. Our code is
available at https://github.com/zju-pi/diff-sampler.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: This paper proposes the Approximate MEan-Direction Solver (AMED-Solver), a new single-step ODE solver for diffusion models aimed at minimizing discretization errors in sampling. It shows significant improvements in sample quality with only around 5 function evaluations (NFE), achieving state-of-the-art Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) scores on various datasets.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The AMED-Solver learns to predict mean directions for fast diffusion sampling, leveraging the geometric observation that sampling trajectories lie in a two-dimensional subspace. Additionally, an improvement plugin (AMED-Plugin) is introduced to enhance existing ODE solvers.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted on datasets like CIFAR-10, ImageNet, and LSUN Bedroom, evaluating image synthesis quality at different resolutions (32 to 512). The sampling quality was assessed using FID scores calculated with 50,000 images generated during testing, and several baseline ODE solvers were compared.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The methodology is sensitive to the choice of time schedules, which can affect performance significantly. Fixed schedules may not work optimally across all scenarios, indicating a need for better design in future use.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work could focus on exploring adaptive time scheduling methods and further refining the geometric understanding of sampling trajectories in diffusion models.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title:
Carve3D: Improving Multi-view Reconstruction Consistency for Diffusion
  Models with RL Finetuning
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
Multi-view diffusion models, obtained by applying Supervised Finetuning (SFT)
to text-to-image diffusion models, have driven recent breakthroughs in
text-to-3D research. However, due to the limited size and quality of existing
3D datasets, they still suffer from multi-view inconsistencies and Neural
Radiance Field (NeRF) reconstruction artifacts. We argue that multi-view
diffusion models can benefit from further Reinforcement Learning Finetuning
(RLFT), which allows models to learn from the data generated by themselves and
improve beyond their dataset limitations during SFT. To this end, we introduce
Carve3D, an improved RLFT algorithm coupled with a novel Multi-view
Reconstruction Consistency (MRC) metric, to enhance the consistency of
multi-view diffusion models. To measure the MRC metric on a set of multi-view
images, we compare them with their corresponding NeRF renderings at the same
camera viewpoints. The resulting model, which we denote as Carve3DM,
demonstrates superior multi-view consistency and NeRF reconstruction quality
than existing models. Our results suggest that pairing SFT with Carve3D's RLFT
is essential for developing multi-view-consistent diffusion models, mirroring
the standard Large Language Model (LLM) alignment pipeline. Our code, training
and testing data, and video results are available at:
https://desaixie.github.io/carve-3d.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The research presents Carve3D, a new reinforcement learning finetuning (RLFT) algorithm aimed at improving multi-view consistency in diffusion models, addressing issues of inconsistency and artifacts in existing models caused by limited 3D datasets. The introduction of a Multi-view Reconstruction Consistency (MRC) metric facilitates evaluation and enhancement of multi-view image consistency, demonstrating Carve3DM improves upon previous models both quantitatively and qualitatively.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: Carve3D employs an RLFT approach, using a policy gradient algorithm to finetune a pre-trained multi-view diffusion model based on generated images and their MRC rewards. The method leverages the Large Reconstruction Model (LRM) for rapid NeRF reconstruction and applies the perceptual LPIPS metric for evaluating image consistency. It incorporates KL divergence regularization to mitigate distribution shifts during finetuning.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: The study utilizes the Instant3D-10K dataset as a base multi-view diffusion model, employing a custom set of curated text prompts for training, each producing multiple diverse outputs to evaluate MRC. Experiments consist of quantitative assessments comparing Carve3DM against models like Instant3D with varying degrees of supervised fine-tuning steps (10K, 20K, 100K) and qualitative user studies measuring 3D consistency and prompt alignment on select prompts.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The Carve3D approach is limited by the quality and fidelity of the Sparse View Large Reconstruction Model used for NeRF, which may negatively impact high-frequency details in generated outputs. Additionally, the scalability of the method is constrained by the computational demands during training and the size of the 3D datasets, which restricts broader applicability to diverse models.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future work may explore extending the MRC metric to other generative models, improving the framework's efficiency through alternative algorithms or reconstruction techniques such as Consistency Models, and further optimizing data preparation and scaling strategies to enhance training capability without degrading output quality.

selected_add_paper_info_list.title: Shadow Generation for Composite Image Using Diffusion model
selected_add_paper_info_list.summary:
In the realm of image composition, generating realistic shadow for the
inserted foreground remains a formidable challenge. Previous works have
developed image-to-image translation models which are trained on paired
training data. However, they are struggling to generate shadows with accurate
shapes and intensities, hindered by data scarcity and inherent task complexity.
In this paper, we resort to foundation model with rich prior knowledge of
natural shadow images. Specifically, we first adapt ControlNet to our task and
then propose intensity modulation modules to improve the shadow intensity.
Moreover, we extend the small-scale DESOBA dataset to DESOBAv2 using a novel
data acquisition pipeline. Experimental results on both DESOBA and DESOBAv2
datasets as well as real composite images demonstrate the superior capability
of our model for shadow generation task. The dataset, code, and model are
released at https://github.com/bcmi/Object-Shadow-Generation-Dataset-DESOBAv2.
selected_add_paper_info_list.main_contributions: The research addresses the challenge of generating realistic shadows for inserted foreground objects in composite images. It introduces a novel shadow generation method using a diffusion model, adapted from ControlNet, and presents a large-scale shadow generation dataset, DESOBAv2, which significantly enhances the number of real image pairs for training.
selected_add_paper_info_list.methodology: The methodology involves adapting ControlNet for shadow generation by modifying its control encoder input and integrating intensity modulation modules to enhance shadow intensity. The proposed SGDiffusion model also uses a post-processing network to rectify color shifts and background variations.
selected_add_paper_info_list.experimental_setup: Experiments were conducted using two datasets: the original DESOBA dataset and a larger DESOBAv2 dataset with 21,575 real images and 28,573 pairs. Evaluation metrics included RMSE and SSIM for image quality, and Balanced Error Rate (BER) for mask accuracy. The approach compared results against baseline methods for both quantitative and qualitative analysis.
selected_add_paper_info_list.limitations: The research does not mention specific limitations in terms of model applicability to all scenarios or the potential biases introduced by the training datasets. However, some issues with generating shadows for complex or transparent objects were noted, indicating challenges in generalization in certain cases.
selected_add_paper_info_list.future_research_directions: Future research could explore enhancements in shadow generation for more complex scenes, better handling of transparency and occlusion in shadows, and the development of additional datasets to improve model robustness and address the varied lighting conditions.

base_method_text:
{"arxiv_id":"2310.08529v3","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2310.08529v3","title":"GaussianDreamer: Fast Generation from Text to 3D Gaussians by Bridging
  2D and 3D Diffusion Models","authors":["Taoran Yi","Jiemin Fang","Junjie Wang","Guanjun Wu","Lingxi Xie","Xiaopeng Zhang","Wenyu Liu","Qi Tian","Xinggang Wang"],"published_date":"2023-10-12T17:22:24Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"In recent times, the generation of 3D assets from text prompts has shown
impressive results. Both 2D and 3D diffusion models can help generate decent 3D
objects based on prompts. 3D diffusion models have good 3D consistency, but
their quality and generalization are limited as trainable 3D data is expensive
and hard to obtain. 2D diffusion models enjoy strong abilities of
generalization and fine generation, but 3D consistency is hard to guarantee.
This paper attempts to bridge the power from the two types of diffusion models
via the recent explicit and efficient 3D Gaussian splatting representation. A
fast 3D object generation framework, named as GaussianDreamer, is proposed,
where the 3D diffusion model provides priors for initialization and the 2D
diffusion model enriches the geometry and appearance. Operations of noisy point
growing and color perturbation are introduced to enhance the initialized
Gaussians. Our GaussianDreamer can generate a high-quality 3D instance or 3D
avatar within 15 minutes on one GPU, much faster than previous methods, while
the generated instances can be directly rendered in real time. Demos and code
are available at https://taoranyi.com/gaussiandreamer/.","github_url":"https://github.com/threestudio-project/threestudio","main_contributions":"The paper presents GaussianDreamer, a method that efficiently bridges 3D and 2D diffusion models using Gaussian splatting. It significantly enhances the generation of 3D assets from text prompts, achieving real-time rendering and maintaining high-quality outputs while reducing training time to just 15 minutes on a single GPU.","methodology":"GaussianDreamer employs a framework that utilizes both 3D diffusion models for initial Gaussian point generation and 2D diffusion models to enrich the appearance and geometry of 3D instances. It incorporates noisy point growing and color perturbation for initialization, followed by interaction with the 2D model via Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) for optimization.","experimental_setup":"The method was evaluated using benchmarks like T3Bench, comparing performance metrics against other state-of-the-art methods in text-to-3D generation. The experiments demonstrated the quality of generated 3D assets under varying complexities in prompts, with a focus on both single-object generation and multi-object scenarios.","limitations":"The generated 3D assets sometimes lack sharp edges and may have unnecessary 3D Gaussians, posing challenges in the filtering process. Additionally, multi-face problems may still occur for objects with minimal geometric differences but significant appearance variations. The method also struggles with generating large-scale scenes.","future_research_directions":"Future work could explore improvements in the filtering techniques for 3D Gaussian generation, enhancing the method’s ability to handle complex scenes, and integrating 3D-aware diffusion models to overcome multi-face issues. Expanding the application scope to produce dynamic 3D assets from text prompts could also be a promising avenue."}

new_method:
Below is the outcome of step 3: a novel method inspired by the Base Method and by an approach drawn from the Add Method (specifically, DiffAssemble):

─────────────────────────────  
Proposed Method: Graph-GaussianAssembler

Overview:
Graph-GaussianAssembler is a new framework for text-to-3D asset generation that not only initializes and enriches 3D Gaussian splats from text prompts (as in GaussianDreamer) but also “reassembles” and refines the collection of Gaussians using a graph-diffusion network. By representing individual Gaussians as nodes in a spatial graph, the method leverages iterative graph denoising and reassembly—drawing inspiration from DiffAssemble’s guided reconstruction—to mitigate common issues such as redundant Gaussians, blurred or overly smooth edges, and the multi-face phenomenon.

Key Innovations:
1. Graph-based Reassembly of Gaussians:
  • Rather than treating the placement of Gaussians purely as independent points refined through noisy point growth and color perturbation, the method represents each Gaussian as a node in a graph.
  • A dedicated Graph Neural Network (GNN) module is trained to iteratively “denoise” and reassemble the Gaussians, learning attention-based affinities between nodes. This promotes coherent merging of redundant Gaussians, sharpens boundaries, and enforces consistent poses.
  • The network leverages a diffusion-inspired formulation: analogous to DiffAssemble’s iterative denoising, the GNN takes noisy Gaussian configurations and iteratively outputs refined positions, orientations, and scales.

2. Joint 2D-3D Iterative Refinement:
  • In a two-stage process, the method begins with a fast initialization from a 3D diffusion prior (as in GaussianDreamer) to get a rough set of Gaussians.
  • An auxiliary 2D diffusion model then enriches texture and appearance details via a modified Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) that works in tandem with the graph module.
  • The iterative interplay between the graph reassembly and 2D refinement helps align the geometry more accurately with text-driven appearance cues while resolving multi-face artifacts.

3. Adaptive Filtering and Multi-Scale Consistency:
  • The graph approach naturally identifies clusters of Gaussians that are overly noisy or redundant. A learnable adaptive filtering mechanism, integrated into the GNN, prunes unnecessary or interfering Gaussians.
  • Moreover, the method incorporates multi-scale analysis: coarser graph nodes represent large-scale structure while finer nodes capture detail, ensuring both global consistency and local sharpness.

4. Flexible Time Scheduling via Learned Graph Denoising:
  • To counter issues sensitive to fixed procedural schedules (a challenge noted in both methods), the Graph-GaussianAssembler employs an adaptive time step selection in its denoising process. This dynamic scheduling is learned during training to optimally adjust to different scene complexities and text prompt variations.

Mitigated Challenges:
• Redundant and Unnecessary Gaussians: The graph-based reassembly condenses the Gaussian set by learning affinities and merging overlapping elements.
• Blurry or Undefined Edges: Graph-guided localized denoising sharpens object boundaries and reinforces geometrical consistency, addressing issues of fuzzy edges.
• Multi-face Inconsistencies: By encoding spatial relationships across Gaussians, the method differentiates between subtle geometric differences and prevents the emergence of superfluous faces.
• Scalability to Complex Scenes: The multi-scale graph formulation allows the method to handle larger scenes by working hierarchically, reducing computational overhead without sacrificing detail.

In summary, Graph-GaussianAssembler is a conceptually new framework that blends the rapid text-to-3D generation ability of GaussianDreamer with the graph-based, diffusion-inspired reassembly power from DiffAssemble. This synthesis addresses several key limitations of the Base Method, most notably by ensuring cleaner, more consistent, and geometrically faithful 3D assets with sharp, well-defined boundaries—all achieved in an efficient, iterative manner.

─────────────────────────────  
This completes step 3 as required.
