verification_policy:
Below is a high-level experimental plan that includes three concrete, realistic experiments to demonstrate the superiority of the Graph-GaussianAssembler over baseline methods.

─────────────────────────────  
Experiment 1: End-to-End Comparison on Standard Text-to-3D Benchmarks

Objective: Demonstrate that the full Graph-GaussianAssembler framework produces 3D assets that are more geometrically faithful, have sharper boundaries, and avoid redundant or “multi-face” artifacts compared to baseline approaches (e.g., GaussianDreamer and DiffAssemble).

Design:
• Prepare a standardized set of text prompts (or use an existing benchmark dataset for text-to-3D asset generation).
• For each text prompt, generate 3D assets using:
  – The baseline method (e.g., GaussianDreamer),
  – A reconstruction variant resembling DiffAssemble, and
  – The full Graph-GaussianAssembler.
• Render images of the resulting 3D assets from consistent viewpoints.
• Quantitatively assess the outputs using several metrics:
  – Edge sharpness and structural fidelity (e.g., using a combination of Chamfer Distance on the point clouds and SSIM/PSNR on rendered images),
  – Redundancy measurements, for instance by computing Gaussian density across spatial regions to assess overlapping/redundant Gaussians,
  – Artifact detection (e.g., counting wrongly placed “faces” or extraneous surface patches via geometric consistency checks).
• Qualitative user studies on perceptual realism may be incorporated, where human raters compare the outputs.
• Implementation can be done in Python using PyTorch for model training and differentiable rendering libraries (e.g., PyTorch3D) for visualization/metric computation.

─────────────────────────────  
Experiment 2: Ablation Study on the Graph-Based Reassembly Module

Objective: Isolate and quantify the contribution of the graph-diffusion module in correcting redundant Gaussians and improving edge sharpness.

Design:
• Create at least two variants of the Graph-GaussianAssembler:
  – The complete method with the graph-based reassembly (full GNN denoising module) and
  – An ablated version wherein the graph module is removed or replaced with a simple aggregation (e.g., independent denoising for each Gaussian).
• For both variants, run the asset generation on a controlled subset of prompts that challenge edge precision and object consistency.
• Measure:
  – The degree of redundancy (by assessing the clustering or overlap of Gaussian nodes),
  – Refinement quality of boundaries (using local gradient measures in the rendered images),
  – Overall geometric consistency (via metrics such as Chamfer Distance between the generated Gaussian set and a ground-truth or high-fidelity surrogate model).
• This ablation study can be implemented in Python using frameworks like PyTorch Geometric to easily switch between the attention-based GNN and simpler per-node operations.

─────────────────────────────  
Experiment 3: Evaluation of Adaptive Time Scheduling in Graph Denoising

Objective: Validate that the learned adaptive time scheduling in the graph denoising phase leads to faster convergence, improved stability, and better handling of scene complexity compared to fixed schedules.

Design:
• Implement two versions of the denoising process:
  – The proposed adaptive time scheduling variant, where the schedule is learned based on scene complexity,
  – A baseline variant using a fixed procedural schedule (as typically used in GaussianDreamer/DiffAssemble).
• For a set of text prompts with varying complexity (from simple to complex geometries), run both variants and record:
  – The convergence speed (e.g., number of iterations needed until a stability metric in the Gaussian parameters is reached),
  – The quality of the result after a fixed computation budget (using similar metrics as above: boundary sharpness, redundancy, geometric consistency),
  – Computational efficiency (e.g., total runtime, memory usage).
• This comparison can be implemented in Python by incorporating dynamic vs. static scheduling policies into the iterative graph denoising loop and logging convergence metrics. Visualization of the denoising trajectories (e.g., changes in Gaussian positions over iterations) can provide additional insights.

─────────────────────────────  
Coding Feasibility:
• Each experiment can be implemented in Python using PyTorch as the primary deep learning framework.
• Graph functionality (e.g., for the GNN module) can come from libraries like PyTorch Geometric or DGL.
• Differentiable rendering libraries (such as PyTorch3D) can be leveraged for 3D asset rendering and metric computation.
• Standard Python libraries (NumPy, SciPy, matplotlib) can assist with data handling and result visualization.

With this plan, you can quantitatively and qualitatively demonstrate that Graph-GaussianAssembler improves over the baselines by reducing redundant Gaussians, sharpening edges and boundaries, and adapting the denoising process to scene complexity—all of which are feasible to code and test in Python.
experiment_details:
Below is a detailed experimental plan that meets the Verification Policy requirements. The experimental design is organized into three main experiments (with code snippets illustrating implementation in PyTorch and auxiliary libraries) and is structured so that each experiment emphasizes different aspects of our Graph-GaussianAssembler. In each experiment, we use existing Python libraries (PyTorch, PyTorch3D, PyTorch Geometric, NumPy, etc.) and follow a design that is as close as possible to the experimental information in the original research.

─────────────────────────────  
EXPERIMENT 1: END-TO-END COMPARISON ON STANDARD TEXT-TO-3D BENCHMARKS

Objective:
– Demonstrate that the full Graph-GaussianAssembler framework produces 3D assets with higher geometric fidelity, sharper boundaries, and fewer redundant “multi-face” artifacts compared to baseline methods such as GaussianDreamer and a reconstruction variant akin to DiffAssemble.
  
Design:
1. Data & Prompt Selection  
  • Create a standardized set of text prompts. (Optionally, leverage existing public datasets or benchmark prompts used in similar works.)  
  • Use random camera views for rendering to align with the original research’s method.

2. Methods to Compare  
  • Baseline: GaussianDreamer (using available configs, e.g., guasiandreamer.yaml).  
  • Reconstruction Variant: A variant that replicates DiffAssemble’s reconstruction style.  
  • Full Method: Graph-GaussianAssembler with the complete graph-based reassembly and adaptive denoising schedule.

3. 3D Asset Generation and Rendering  
  • For each prompt, generate 3D assets using the three methods.  
  • Use differentiable rendering (e.g., via PyTorch3D) to render images from consistent viewpoints.  
  • Logging and saving intermediate Gaussian parameters as needed.

4. Evaluation Metrics  
  • Edge sharpness/structural fidelity:  
   - Compute Chamfer Distance between corresponding point clouds converted from Gaussian splatting outputs and reference models (or high-fidelity reconstructions).  
   - Measure rendered image quality using SSIM/PSNR.  
  • Redundancy analysis:  
   - Compute local Gaussian density and measure clustering or overlapping Gaussians.  
  • Artifact detection:  
   - Use geometric consistency checks (e.g., detect extra surfaces or “multi-faces” using edge detection or clustering methods).  
  • Qualitative Study:  
   - Optionally, perform a user study where human raters compare the realism and coherence of the generated assets.

5. Example Code Outline (Experiment 1):

-------------------------------------------------
# Libraries for deep learning, rendering, and metrics
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pytorch3d.renderer import FoVPerspectiveCameras, RasterizationSettings, MeshRenderer, MeshRasterizer, SoftPhongShader
from pytorch3d.structures import Meshes
# Assumption: The code for GaussianDreamer and DiffAssemble variants is available as modules.
from gaussian_dreamer import GaussianDreamer
from diff_assemble import DiffAssemble
from graph_gaussian_assembler import GraphGaussianAssembler

# A dummy function to convert a Gaussian representation into a 3D point cloud or mesh (for metric computations)
def convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(gaussians):
    # In practice, this would take the set of Gaussians (positions, sizes, etc.)
    # and sample points from their distributions to build a point cloud or generate a mesh.
    # Here, we use a stub.
    return np.random.rand(1000, 3)

# Define a function for computing Chamfer Distance (or load from external library)
def compute_chamfer_distance(pc1, pc2):
    # Here a placeholder implementation is provided.
    return np.abs(np.mean(pc1)-np.mean(pc2))

# A simplified pipeline for one prompt
def run_pipeline(prompt: str):
    # Initialize three methods
    baseline_model = GaussianDreamer(config_path="/home/ubuntu/repos/threestudio-gaussiandreamer/configs/gaussiandreamer.yaml")
    recon_variant = DiffAssemble(config_path="/home/ubuntu/repos/threestudio-gaussiandreamer/configs/gaussiandreamer_mvdream.yaml")
    full_model = GraphGaussianAssembler()  # uses our full graph-based reassembly and adaptive scheduling

    # Generate assets
    asset_baseline = baseline_model.generate_3d_asset(prompt)
    asset_recon = recon_variant.generate_3d_asset(prompt)
    asset_full = full_model.generate_3d_asset(prompt)

    # Convert assets to point clouds for metric computations
    pc_baseline = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_baseline)
    pc_recon = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_recon)
    pc_full = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_full)

    # Compare Chamfer Distance between assets (or against a surrogate model if available)
    chamfer_baseline = compute_chamfer_distance(pc_baseline, pc_full)
    chamfer_recon = compute_chamfer_distance(pc_recon, pc_full)
    print(f"Chamfer Distance: Baseline vs Full={chamfer_baseline:.3f}, Recon vs Full={chamfer_recon:.3f}")

    # Render images using PyTorch3D (example for one asset)
    cameras = FoVPerspectiveCameras(device="cuda")
    raster_settings = RasterizationSettings(image_size=256, blur_radius=0.0, faces_per_pixel=1)
    renderer = MeshRenderer(
        rasterizer=MeshRasterizer(cameras=cameras, raster_settings=raster_settings),
        shader=SoftPhongShader(device="cuda", cameras=cameras)
    )
    # Assuming a conversion function using Open3D or custom conversion from Gaussians to Meshes
    mesh_full = convert_gaussians_to_mesh(asset_full)
    images = renderer(mesh_full.extend(1))
    # Compute SSIM/PSNR metrics against a ground-truth or compare rendered outputs
    # (implementation omitted)

    # Optionally, save images to disk for qualitative analysis.

# Example run for several prompts
prompts = ["a futuristic city", "a medieval castle", "a natural landscape"]
for prompt in prompts:
    run_pipeline(prompt)
-------------------------------------------------

Notes:
• The functions generate_3d_asset, convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud, and convert_gaussians_to_mesh are assumed to interface with the existing code base.
• Metrics for boundary sharpness (SSIM, PSNR) can be computed using libraries like scikit-image.

─────────────────────────────  
EXPERIMENT 2: ABLATION STUDY ON THE GRAPH-BASED REASSEMBLY MODULE

Objective:
– Isolate the contribution of the graph-based reassembly (i.e., the GNN denoising module that corrects overlapping and redundant Gaussians) compared to a simpler, non-graph-based aggregation/de-noising variant.

Design:
1. Model Variants  
  • Full Graph-GaussianAssembler with an attention-based GNN module for node (Gaussian) reassembly.  
  • Ablated Variant: Replace the GNN module with a per-node independent denoising function (e.g., a simple MLP applied to each Gaussian independently).

2. Evaluation Procedure  
  • Run the asset generation on a controlled subset of challenging prompts (with complex edges and structural consistency requirements).  
  • Compute the following metrics:  
   - Redundancy degree: Use clustering analysis on the Gaussian centers, quantify overlapping using pairwise distance metrics, or compute density measures.  
   - Boundary refinement: Measure local gradients in the rendered images and compare with ground-truth edges (for example, a Sobel edge detector followed by quantitative evaluation).  
   - Overall geometric consistency: Use Chamfer Distance between Gaussian-generated point clouds and a reference high-fidelity model.

3. Example Implementation Outline:

-------------------------------------------------
import torch_geometric
from torch_geometric.nn import GATConv  # For a generic graph attention layer

# Define a simple GNN module for the full method
class GraphDenoisingModule(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels):
        super(GraphDenoisingModule, self).__init__()
        self.conv1 = GATConv(in_channels, 64)
        self.conv2 = GATConv(64, out_channels)

    def forward(self, x, edge_index):
        # x: node features, edge_index: connectivity in COO format.
        x = torch.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index))
        return self.conv2(x, edge_index)

# Define an ablated per-node denoising module (no graph interactions)
class IndependentDenoisingModule(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels):
        super(IndependentDenoisingModule, self).__init__()
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(in_channels, 64),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(64, out_channels)
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.mlp(x)

# Integration in the asset generation pipeline
def generate_asset_variant(prompt: str, use_graph: bool = True):
    if use_graph:
        denoise_module = GraphDenoisingModule(in_channels=16, out_channels=16)
        model = GraphGaussianAssembler(denoise_module=denoise_module)
    else:
        denoise_module = IndependentDenoisingModule(in_channels=16, out_channels=16)
        model = GraphGaussianAssembler(denoise_module=denoise_module)
    asset = model.generate_3d_asset(prompt)
    return asset

# Run on a subset of prompts and measure metrics (reuse metric functions from Experiment 1)
test_prompts = ["complex mechanical part", "intricate sculpture"]
for prompt in test_prompts:
    asset_full = generate_asset_variant(prompt, use_graph=True)
    asset_simple = generate_asset_variant(prompt, use_graph=False)
    # Convert to point clouds
    pc_full = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_full)
    pc_simple = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_simple)
    redundancy_metric_full = compute_redundancy_metric(pc_full)
    redundancy_metric_simple = compute_redundancy_metric(pc_simple)
    print(f"Prompt: {prompt}")
    print(f"Redundancy (Graph Module): {redundancy_metric_full}, (Simple Module): {redundancy_metric_simple}")
-------------------------------------------------

Notes:
• The function compute_redundancy_metric could be implemented based on clustering or density estimation (e.g., using simple-knn or scikit-learn clustering).
• By comparing outputs, we can quantitatively assess how much the graph module reduces redundant Gaussians and improves boundary quality.

─────────────────────────────  
EXPERIMENT 3: EVALUATION OF ADAPTIVE TIME SCHEDULING IN GRAPH DENOISING

Objective:
– Validate that the learned adaptive time scheduling during the denoising phase improves convergence speed, stability, and overall quality (e.g., better handling of scene complexity) compared to a fixed/invariant scheduling approach.

Design:
1. Implementation Variants  
  • Adaptive Scheduling: Integrate a learnable module that adjusts the denoising time steps based on scene complexity metrics (for instance, complexity measured as variance or entropy of the Gaussian distributions).  
  • Fixed Scheduling: Use a standard fixed schedule, as implemented in GaussianDreamer or DiffAssemble.

2. Measurement Criteria  
  • Convergence Speed:  
   - Record the number of iterations until the change in Gaussian parameters falls below a threshold.  
  • Final Quality After Fixed Budget:  
   - Use boundary sharpness (local gradient metrics), redundancy measures, and geometric consistency (Chamfer Distance) for evaluation.  
  • Computational Efficiency:  
   - Log runtime per iteration and memory consumption.
   - Visualize the evolution of Gaussian parameters over iterations (a “denoising trajectory plot”).

3. Example Code Outline:

-------------------------------------------------
# Example of an adaptive time scheduler module
class AdaptiveTimeScheduler(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, init_steps=50):
        super(AdaptiveTimeScheduler, self).__init__()
        # A small network that predicts adjustment based on scene complexity.
        self.fc = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(1, 10),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(10, 1)
        )
        self.init_steps = init_steps

    def forward(self, complexity_measure):
        # complexity_measure could be the current variance of the Gaussian parameters.
        adjustment = self.fc(complexity_measure.unsqueeze(0))
        # Dynamic schedule: e.g., modify the number of iterations.
        adaptive_steps = self.init_steps - int(adjustment.item())
        return max(10, adaptive_steps)  # Ensure a minimum of iterations

def denoising_process(model, asset, adaptive_schedule: bool = True):
    # asset contains initial Gaussian parameters among other info.
    num_iterations_fixed = 50  # For fixed schedule
    current_asset = asset
    iteration = 0
    while True:
        # Compute a complexity measure (e.g., variance of Gaussian positions)
        gauss_positions = current_asset['positions']
        complexity_measure = torch.var(gauss_positions)
        # Decide on the number of iterations for the next update cycle
        if adaptive_schedule:
            scheduler = AdaptiveTimeScheduler(init_steps=num_iterations_fixed)
            num_iterations = scheduler(complexity_measure.unsqueeze(0))
        else:
            num_iterations = num_iterations_fixed
        # Perform the denoising update num_iterations times
        for _ in range(num_iterations):
            current_asset = model.denoise_step(current_asset)
            iteration += 1
        # A simple convergence check (e.g., change below a threshold)
        change = torch.norm(current_asset['positions'] - asset['positions'])
        asset = current_asset
        if change < 1e-3 or iteration > 200:  # Stop condition can be refined.
            break
    return current_asset, iteration

# Run experiments on prompts with varying scene complexity.
prompts = ["simple bowl", "busy marketplace", "dense forest scene"]
for prompt in prompts:
    # Initialize the model and asset generation (e.g., using GraphGaussianAssembler)
    model = GraphGaussianAssembler()
    asset_initial = model.initialize_asset(prompt)
    
    # Run with adaptive scheduling
    asset_adaptive, iters_adaptive = denoising_process(model, asset_initial, adaptive_schedule=True)
    # Run with fixed schedule
    asset_fixed, iters_fixed = denoising_process(model, asset_initial, adaptive_schedule=False)
    
    print(f"Prompt: {prompt}")
    print(f"Adaptive Scheduler: iterations = {iters_adaptive}")
    print(f"Fixed Scheduler: iterations = {iters_fixed}")
    
    # Evaluate the final asset quality using previously defined metrics.
    pc_adaptive = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_adaptive)
    pc_fixed = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_fixed)
    chamfer_adaptive = compute_chamfer_distance(pc_adaptive, pc_adaptive)  # Replace with reference if available.
    chamfer_fixed = compute_chamfer_distance(pc_fixed, pc_fixed)
    print(f"Chamfer distances -> Adaptive: {chamfer_adaptive:.3f}, Fixed: {chamfer_fixed:.3f}")
-------------------------------------------------

Notes:
• In practice, the adaptive scheduler’s complexity measure might be computed over multiple statistics of the asset (positions, orientations, etc.).
• Visual logs (e.g., plotting Gaussian positions over iterations) can be generated using matplotlib to provide additional insight.

─────────────────────────────  
OVERALL EXPERIMENTAL CONSIDERATIONS

• We use PyTorch as the deep learning backbone and leverage differentiable rendering (PyTorch3D) and graph processing (PyTorch Geometric) to adhere closely to the original implementation guidelines.  
• Standard python libraries (NumPy, SciPy, matplotlib) support dataset handling, clustering, and result visualization.  
• Each experiment is designed to be modular so that experiments 1, 2, and 3 do not overlap unnecessarily but also share common metric computations where appropriate.  
• For reproducibility, configurations will be saved (e.g., yaml files) and random seeds will be set across libraries.
• The experiments can be extended with qualitative user studies by collecting rendered outputs and surveying human raters on perceived realism and artifact presence.

By following these detailed experimental designs and example implementations in PyTorch, we can thoroughly evaluate and verify that the Graph-GaussianAssembler outperforms baseline methods in terms of geometric fidelity, edge sharpness, artifact reduction, and adaptive denoising efficiency.
experiment_code:
Below is an end‐to‐end Python script that implements the three experiments as described. (Before running, please make sure you have installed the following libraries: torch, torchvision, numpy, matplotlib, pytorch3d, torch_geometric, scikit-image, and any other dependencies such as diff-gaussian-rasterization, simple-knn, and open3d if needed.) 

In this code we provide stub implementations for the three methods (GaussianDreamer, DiffAssemble, and GraphGaussianAssembler) and dummy functions (e.g. for asset conversion, redundancy metric, and chamfer computation). In an actual implementation you would replace these with your real data pipelines and models. The code includes print statements to trace progress, plots the results (and saves them as high-quality PDF figures) and contains a test function that runs very briefly to check that the code executes. 

Below is the complete code:

------------------------------------------------------------
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Required Python Libraries:
 • torch
 • numpy
 • matplotlib
 • pytorch3d
 • torch_geometric
 • scikit-image
 
Additional packages (if available/needed): 
 • diff-gaussian-rasterization, simple-knn, open3d, pymeshlab (optional)
 
This script implements three experiments to evaluate the Graph-GaussianAssembler framework.
"""

import os
import time
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# PyTorch3D imports for rendering support
from pytorch3d.renderer import (
    FoVPerspectiveCameras,
    RasterizationSettings,
    MeshRenderer,
    MeshRasterizer,
    SoftPhongShader
)
from pytorch3d.structures import Meshes

# PyTorch Geometric import for graph modules
from torch_geometric.nn import GATConv  # Graph Attention Convolution

# For image quality metrics (SSIM, PSNR) and edge detection
from skimage.metrics import structural_similarity as ssim
from skimage.metrics import peak_signal_noise_ratio as psnr
from skimage.filters import sobel

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Dummy implementations for GaussianDreamer, DiffAssemble and GraphGaussianAssembler

class GaussianDreamer:
    def __init__(self, config_path):
        self.config_path = config_path
        print(f"Initialized GaussianDreamer with config: {config_path}")
    
    def generate_3d_asset(self, prompt: str):
        # Dummy generation (replace with diffusion and Gaussian splatting code)
        print(f"[GaussianDreamer] Generating 3D asset for prompt: '{prompt}'")
        asset = {"positions": torch.randn(100, 3)}   # 100 Gaussians with random 3D centers
        return asset
    
class DiffAssemble:
    def __init__(self, config_path):
        self.config_path = config_path
        print(f"Initialized DiffAssemble with config: {config_path}")
    
    def generate_3d_asset(self, prompt: str):
        print(f"[DiffAssemble] Generating 3D asset for prompt: '{prompt}'")
        asset = {"positions": torch.randn(110, 3)}   # Slightly different number for demonstration
        return asset

# The GraphGaussianAssembler takes a denoising module.
class GraphGaussianAssembler:
    def __init__(self, denoise_module: nn.Module = None):
        if denoise_module is None:
            # Provide a default dummy module
            self.denoise_module = nn.Identity()
            print("[GraphGaussianAssembler] Using default identity denoising module.")
        else:
            self.denoise_module = denoise_module
            print("[GraphGaussianAssembler] Using provided denoising module.")
    
    def generate_3d_asset(self, prompt: str):
        # Dummy asset generation that calls the denoising module on random Gaussian features.
        print(f"[GraphGaussianAssembler] Generating 3D asset for prompt: '{prompt}'")
        asset = {"positions": torch.randn(105, 3)}  # 105 Gaussians for demonstration
        # A dummy denoising step on the positions (simulate graph-based reassembly)
        asset["positions"] = self.denoise_module(asset["positions"])
        return asset

    def initialize_asset(self, prompt: str):
        # Initialize asset parameters (for Experiment 3) without denoising
        print(f"[GraphGaussianAssembler] Initializing asset for prompt: '{prompt}'")
        asset = {"positions": torch.randn(105, 3)}
        return asset

    # Dummy denoising step for adaptive scheduling experiments.
    def denoise_step(self, asset):
        # For this demo, we perturb the positions slightly to simulate an update.
        new_positions = asset["positions"] - 0.01 * torch.randn_like(asset["positions"])
        return {"positions": new_positions}

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Supporting Functions

def convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset) -> np.ndarray:
    """
    Dummy function that converts asset (Gaussian parameters) to a point cloud.
    In practice, this samples points from the Gaussian distributions.
    """
    positions = asset["positions"].cpu().numpy() if isinstance(asset["positions"], torch.Tensor) else asset["positions"]
    # For simplicity, return positions (optionally add noise or sampling here)
    n_points = positions.shape[0] * 10  # Arbitrarily sample more points for cloud
    pointcloud = np.repeat(positions, 10, axis=0) + np.random.randn(n_points, 3) * 0.005
    return pointcloud

def convert_gaussians_to_mesh(asset) -> Meshes:
    """
    Dummy conversion from Gaussians to a Mesh.
    Here we create a simple mesh from the point cloud positions by treating them as vertices.
    For faces, we use a convex hull placeholder (or simply a dummy connectivity).
    """
    pointcloud = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset)
    vertices = torch.tensor(pointcloud, dtype=torch.float32)
    # Dummy faces: create triangles connecting sequential vertices
    n = vertices.shape[0]
    if n < 3:
        faces = torch.empty((0, 3), dtype=torch.int64)
    else:
        faces = torch.tensor([[i, (i+1)%n, (i+2)%n] for i in range(n-2)], dtype=torch.int64)
    mesh = Meshes(verts=[vertices], faces=[faces])
    return mesh

def compute_chamfer_distance(pc1: np.ndarray, pc2: np.ndarray) -> float:
    """
    Dummy Chamfer Distance computation.
    A true implementation would compute bidirectional nearest neighbor distances.
    """
    mdiff = abs(np.mean(pc1) - np.mean(pc2))
    return mdiff

def compute_redundancy_metric(pc: np.ndarray) -> float:
    """
    Dummy function to compute redundancy metric. For example,
    we return an arbitrary density measure (lower is better).
    """
    # Here, we simply return the inverse of the mean pairwise distance (toy example).
    from scipy.spatial.distance import pdist
    if pc.shape[0] < 2:
        return 0.0
    distances = pdist(pc)
    metric = np.mean(distances)
    return metric

def compute_image_quality_metrics(rendered: np.ndarray, reference: np.ndarray):
    """
    Dummy function for computing SSIM and PSNR between rendered image and reference.
    """
    ssim_val = ssim(rendered, reference, multichannel=True)
    psnr_val = psnr(reference, rendered)
    return ssim_val, psnr_val

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Experiment 1: End-to-End Comparison on Standard Text-to-3D Benchmarks

def experiment1():
    print("\n=== Experiment 1: End-to-End Comparison ===")
    
    # Initialize models using dummy config paths
    baseline_model = GaussianDreamer(config_path="/home/ubuntu/repos/threestudio-gaussiandreamer/configs/gaussiandreamer.yaml")
    recon_variant = DiffAssemble(config_path="/home/ubuntu/repos/threestudio-gaussiandreamer/configs/gaussiandreamer_mvdream.yaml")
    full_model = GraphGaussianAssembler()  # Default denoising
    
    # Define a set of prompts
    prompts = ["a futuristic city", "a medieval castle", "a natural landscape"]
    results = []
    
    # Set up PyTorch3D rendering (using CUDA if available)
    device = torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")
    cameras = FoVPerspectiveCameras(device=device)
    raster_settings = RasterizationSettings(image_size=256, blur_radius=0.0, faces_per_pixel=1)
    renderer = MeshRenderer(
        rasterizer=MeshRasterizer(cameras=cameras, raster_settings=raster_settings),
        shader=SoftPhongShader(device=device, cameras=cameras)
    )
    
    for prompt in prompts:
        print(f"\n[Experiment 1] Processing prompt: '{prompt}'")
        asset_baseline = baseline_model.generate_3d_asset(prompt)
        asset_recon = recon_variant.generate_3d_asset(prompt)
        asset_full = full_model.generate_3d_asset(prompt)
        
        # Convert assets to point clouds
        pc_baseline = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_baseline)
        pc_recon    = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_recon)
        pc_full     = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_full)
        
        chamfer_baseline = compute_chamfer_distance(pc_baseline, pc_full)
        chamfer_recon    = compute_chamfer_distance(pc_recon, pc_full)
        
        print(f"Chamfer Distance (Baseline vs Full): {chamfer_baseline:.4f}")
        print(f"Chamfer Distance (Recon vs Full):    {chamfer_recon:.4f}")
        
        # Render the asset from a consistent camera view
        mesh_full = convert_gaussians_to_mesh(asset_full).to(device)
        rendered_image = renderer(mesh_full.extend(1))
        
        # Convert rendered_image to numpy for analysis (assuming rendered_image is of shape [1,H,W,3])
        rendered_np = rendered_image[0, ..., :3].cpu().numpy()
        # Dummy reference image as a constant image (in practice this would be a ground truth render)
        reference_np = np.ones_like(rendered_np) * 0.5
        
        # Compute dummy image quality metrics
        try:
            ssim_val, psnr_val = compute_image_quality_metrics(rendered_np, reference_np)
            print(f"Rendered image quality -> SSIM: {ssim_val:.4f}, PSNR: {psnr_val:.2f}")
        except Exception as e:
            print("Image quality computation skipped (e.g., due to missing multichannel data)", e)
        
        results.append({
            "prompt": prompt,
            "chamfer_baseline": chamfer_baseline,
            "chamfer_recon": chamfer_recon,
            "ssim": ssim_val if 'ssim_val' in locals() else None,
            "psnr": psnr_val if 'psnr_val' in locals() else None
        })
    
    # Plot and save metric comparisons:
    prompts_list = [r["prompt"] for r in results]
    chamfer_baselines = [r["chamfer_baseline"] for r in results]
    chamfer_recons    = [r["chamfer_recon"] for r in results]
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
    plt.plot(prompts_list, chamfer_baselines, marker="o", label="Baseline vs Full")
    plt.plot(prompts_list, chamfer_recons, marker="s", label="Recon vs Full")
    plt.title("Chamfer Distance Comparison")
    plt.xlabel("Prompt")
    plt.ylabel("Chamfer Distance")
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig("experiment1_chamfer_comparison.pdf")
    plt.close()
    print("Experiment 1 plots saved as 'experiment1_chamfer_comparison.pdf'.")

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Experiment 2: Ablation Study on the Graph-Based Reassembly Module

# Define a simple GNN-based denoising module
class GraphDenoisingModule(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels):
        super(GraphDenoisingModule, self).__init__()
        self.conv1 = GATConv(in_channels, 64)
        self.conv2 = GATConv(64, out_channels)
    
    def forward(self, x, edge_index=None):
        # edge_index is a dummy here; in practice it encapsulates graph connectivity.
        x = torch.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index if edge_index is not None else torch.tensor([[0],[0]], dtype=torch.long)))
        return self.conv2(x, edge_index if edge_index is not None else torch.tensor([[0],[0]], dtype=torch.long))

# Define an ablated per-node (independent) denoising module.
class IndependentDenoisingModule(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels):
        super(IndependentDenoisingModule, self).__init__()
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(in_channels, 64),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(64, out_channels)
        )
    
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.mlp(x)

def generate_asset_variant(prompt: str, use_graph: bool = True):
    """
    Generate an asset with either the full graph module or a simple module.
    """
    if use_graph:
        denoise_module = GraphDenoisingModule(in_channels=3, out_channels=3)
        print(f"[Asset Variant] Using Graph-based denoising for prompt '{prompt}'.")
    else:
        denoise_module = IndependentDenoisingModule(in_channels=3, out_channels=3)
        print(f"[Asset Variant] Using Independent (non-graph) denoising for prompt '{prompt}'.")
    model = GraphGaussianAssembler(denoise_module=denoise_module)
    asset = model.generate_3d_asset(prompt)
    return asset

def experiment2():
    print("\n=== Experiment 2: Ablation Study ===")
    test_prompts = ["complex mechanical part", "intricate sculpture"]
    
    for prompt in test_prompts:
        asset_graph = generate_asset_variant(prompt, use_graph=True)
        asset_simple = generate_asset_variant(prompt, use_graph=False)
        
        pc_graph = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_graph)
        pc_simple = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_simple)
        
        redundancy_graph = compute_redundancy_metric(pc_graph)
        redundancy_simple = compute_redundancy_metric(pc_simple)
        
        print(f"\nPrompt: {prompt}")
        print(f"Redundancy Metric (Graph Module): {redundancy_graph:.4f}")
        print(f"Redundancy Metric (Simple Module): {redundancy_simple:.4f}")
    
    # Plot the redundancy metrics for comparison
    labels = test_prompts
    graph_metrics = [compute_redundancy_metric(convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(generate_asset_variant(p, use_graph=True))) for p in test_prompts]
    simple_metrics = [compute_redundancy_metric(convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(generate_asset_variant(p, use_graph=False))) for p in test_prompts]
    
    x = np.arange(len(labels))
    width = 0.35
    plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
    plt.bar(x - width/2, graph_metrics, width, label='Graph Module')
    plt.bar(x + width/2, simple_metrics, width, label='Simple Module')
    plt.xticks(x, labels)
    plt.ylabel("Redundancy Metric")
    plt.title("Ablation Study: Redundancy Comparison")
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.savefig("experiment2_redundancy_comparison.pdf")
    plt.close()
    print("Experiment 2 plots saved as 'experiment2_redundancy_comparison.pdf'.")

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Experiment 3: Evaluation of Adaptive Time Scheduling in Graph Denoising

# Example adaptive time scheduler module
class AdaptiveTimeScheduler(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, init_steps=50):
        super(AdaptiveTimeScheduler, self).__init__()
        # A small network predicting an adjustment based on scene complexity.
        self.fc = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(1, 10),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(10, 1)
        )
        self.init_steps = init_steps
    
    def forward(self, complexity_measure):
        # complexity_measure: a tensor of shape [1]
        adjustment = self.fc(complexity_measure)
        # Compute adaptive steps; here we subtract the adjustment value from initial steps.
        adaptive_steps = self.init_steps - int(adjustment.item())
        return max(10, adaptive_steps)  # Ensure at least 10 iterations

def denoising_process(model, asset, adaptive_schedule: bool = True):
    num_iterations_fixed = 50
    current_asset = asset
    iteration = 0
    iterations_history = []
    positions_history = []
    
    while True:
        gauss_positions = current_asset['positions']
        complexity_measure = torch.tensor([float(torch.var(gauss_positions))])
        if adaptive_schedule:
            scheduler = AdaptiveTimeScheduler(init_steps=num_iterations_fixed)
            num_iterations = scheduler(complexity_measure)
        else:
            num_iterations = num_iterations_fixed
        
        for _ in range(num_iterations):
            current_asset = model.denoise_step(current_asset)
            iteration += 1
            iterations_history.append(iteration)
            # Record mean position norm for simple tracking
            positions_history.append(torch.norm(current_asset['positions']).item())
        
        # Convergence check: stop if update change is below threshold.
        change = torch.norm(current_asset['positions'] - asset['positions'])
        if change < 1e-3 or iteration > 200:
            break
        
        # Update the asset for next cycle
        asset = current_asset
    return current_asset, iteration, iterations_history, positions_history

def experiment3():
    print("\n=== Experiment 3: Adaptive Time Scheduling Evaluation ===")
    prompts = ["simple bowl", "busy marketplace", "dense forest scene"]
    
    for prompt in prompts:
        print(f"\n[Experiment 3] Processing prompt: '{prompt}'")
        model = GraphGaussianAssembler()  # Use default denoising_step
        asset_initial = model.initialize_asset(prompt)
        
        # Run with adaptive scheduling
        asset_adaptive, iters_adaptive, hist_iters_adaptive, hist_pos_adaptive = denoising_process(model, asset_initial, adaptive_schedule=True)
        # Run with fixed scheduling
        asset_fixed, iters_fixed, hist_iters_fixed, hist_pos_fixed = denoising_process(model, asset_initial, adaptive_schedule=False)
        
        print(f"Prompt: {prompt}")
        print(f"Adaptive Scheduler iterations: {iters_adaptive}")
        print(f"Fixed Scheduler iterations:    {iters_fixed}")
        
        # Compute dummy Chamfer distances (here using self-comparison as a placeholder)
        pc_adaptive = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_adaptive)
        pc_fixed = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_fixed)
        chamfer_adaptive = compute_chamfer_distance(pc_adaptive, pc_adaptive)  # In practice compare to a reference
        chamfer_fixed = compute_chamfer_distance(pc_fixed, pc_fixed)
        print(f"Chamfer Distance -> Adaptive: {chamfer_adaptive:.4f}, Fixed: {chamfer_fixed:.4f}")
        
        # Plot and save denoising trajectory (mean norm of positions over iterations)
        plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
        plt.plot(hist_iters_adaptive, hist_pos_adaptive, label="Adaptive Scheduling")
        plt.plot(hist_iters_fixed, hist_pos_fixed, label="Fixed Scheduling", linestyle="--")
        plt.xlabel("Iteration")
        plt.ylabel("Mean Position Norm")
        plt.title(f"Denoising Trajectory for prompt: {prompt}")
        plt.legend()
        plt.tight_layout()
        filename = f"experiment3_trajectory_{prompt.replace(' ', '_')}.pdf"
        plt.savefig(filename)
        plt.close()
        print(f"Denoising trajectory plot saved as '{filename}'.")

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Test function to check that the code executes correctly (run quickly)

def test_code():
    print("\n=== Running quick test... ===")
    
    # Run experiment1 for one prompt only (quick run)
    prompt_test = "quick test prompt"
    baseline_model = GaussianDreamer(config_path="dummy_path.yaml")
    asset = baseline_model.generate_3d_asset(prompt_test)
    pc = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset)
    print(f"Test asset point cloud shape: {pc.shape}")
    
    # Test the denoising modules from Experiment 2
    test_input = torch.randn(10, 3)
    gnn = GraphDenoisingModule(in_channels=3, out_channels=3)
    indep = IndependentDenoisingModule(in_channels=3, out_channels=3)
    out_gnn = gnn(test_input)
    out_indep = indep(test_input)
    print(f"GraphDenoisingModule output shape: {out_gnn.shape}")
    print(f"IndependentDenoisingModule output shape: {out_indep.shape}")
    
    # Test Adaptive Scheduler from Experiment 3
    scheduler = AdaptiveTimeScheduler(init_steps=50)
    dummy_complexity = torch.tensor([0.5])
    steps = scheduler(dummy_complexity)
    print(f"AdaptiveTimeScheduler predicted steps: {steps}")
    
    print("Quick test finished.\n")

# -------------------------------------------------------------------
# Main function to run all experiments

def main():
    start_time = time.time()
    # Uncomment the following lines to run full experiments
    experiment1()
    experiment2()
    experiment3()
    # Run the quick test at the end (this test finishes immediately)
    test_code()
    end_time = time.time()
    print(f"Total runtime: {end_time - start_time:.2f} seconds")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
------------------------------------------------------------

Explanation:

1. Experiment 1 (“End-to-End Comparison”) sets up three dummy models (GaussianDreamer, DiffAssemble, and GraphGaussianAssembler). For each prompt the code generates assets, converts them to point clouds, computes a dummy Chamfer metric, and renders (using PyTorch3D) a dummy image. The resulting metrics are printed, plotted, and saved as PDF.
   
2. Experiment 2 (“Ablation Study”) demonstrates how to replace the full graph-based denoising module with an independent (MLP-based) module. It then computes a dummy redundancy metric (using pairwise distances) and plots the metric comparison.
   
3. Experiment 3 (“Adaptive Time Scheduling”) implements a basic adaptive time scheduler and runs a dummy denoising process. It tracks and plots the evolution of a simple Gaussian parameter norm over iterations for both adaptive and fixed scheduling.

4. The test_code() function at the end is very short and serves to quickly verify that the main components (asset generation, denoising modules, scheduler) are running.

This code is structured to closely follow the experimental information provided in the research and produces verbose standard output and high-quality vector plots saved as PDFs, making it suitable for academic inclusion.

    new_method: Below is the outcome of step 3: a novel method inspired by the Base Method and by an approach drawn from the Add Method (specifically, DiffAssemble):

─────────────────────────────  
Proposed Method: Graph-GaussianAssembler

Overview:
Graph-GaussianAssembler is a new framework for text-to-3D asset generation that not only initializes and enriches 3D Gaussian splats from text prompts (as in GaussianDreamer) but also “reassembles” and refines the collection of Gaussians using a graph-diffusion network. By representing individual Gaussians as nodes in a spatial graph, the method leverages iterative graph denoising and reassembly—drawing inspiration from DiffAssemble’s guided reconstruction—to mitigate common issues such as redundant Gaussians, blurred or overly smooth edges, and the multi-face phenomenon.

Key Innovations:
1. Graph-based Reassembly of Gaussians:
  • Rather than treating the placement of Gaussians purely as independent points refined through noisy point growth and color perturbation, the method represents each Gaussian as a node in a graph.
  • A dedicated Graph Neural Network (GNN) module is trained to iteratively “denoise” and reassemble the Gaussians, learning attention-based affinities between nodes. This promotes coherent merging of redundant Gaussians, sharpens boundaries, and enforces consistent poses.
  • The network leverages a diffusion-inspired formulation: analogous to DiffAssemble’s iterative denoising, the GNN takes noisy Gaussian configurations and iteratively outputs refined positions, orientations, and scales.

2. Joint 2D-3D Iterative Refinement:
  • In a two-stage process, the method begins with a fast initialization from a 3D diffusion prior (as in GaussianDreamer) to get a rough set of Gaussians.
  • An auxiliary 2D diffusion model then enriches texture and appearance details via a modified Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) that works in tandem with the graph module.
  • The iterative interplay between the graph reassembly and 2D refinement helps align the geometry more accurately with text-driven appearance cues while resolving multi-face artifacts.

3. Adaptive Filtering and Multi-Scale Consistency:
  • The graph approach naturally identifies clusters of Gaussians that are overly noisy or redundant. A learnable adaptive filtering mechanism, integrated into the GNN, prunes unnecessary or interfering Gaussians.
  • Moreover, the method incorporates multi-scale analysis: coarser graph nodes represent large-scale structure while finer nodes capture detail, ensuring both global consistency and local sharpness.

4. Flexible Time Scheduling via Learned Graph Denoising:
  • To counter issues sensitive to fixed procedural schedules (a challenge noted in both methods), the Graph-GaussianAssembler employs an adaptive time step selection in its denoising process. This dynamic scheduling is learned during training to optimally adjust to different scene complexities and text prompt variations.

Mitigated Challenges:
• Redundant and Unnecessary Gaussians: The graph-based reassembly condenses the Gaussian set by learning affinities and merging overlapping elements.
• Blurry or Undefined Edges: Graph-guided localized denoising sharpens object boundaries and reinforces geometrical consistency, addressing issues of fuzzy edges.
• Multi-face Inconsistencies: By encoding spatial relationships across Gaussians, the method differentiates between subtle geometric differences and prevents the emergence of superfluous faces.
• Scalability to Complex Scenes: The multi-scale graph formulation allows the method to handle larger scenes by working hierarchically, reducing computational overhead without sacrificing detail.

In summary, Graph-GaussianAssembler is a conceptually new framework that blends the rapid text-to-3D generation ability of GaussianDreamer with the graph-based, diffusion-inspired reassembly power from DiffAssemble. This synthesis addresses several key limitations of the Base Method, most notably by ensuring cleaner, more consistent, and geometrically faithful 3D assets with sharp, well-defined boundaries—all achieved in an efficient, iterative manner.

─────────────────────────────  
This completes step 3 as required.
    
    verification_policy: Below is a high-level experimental plan that includes three concrete, realistic experiments to demonstrate the superiority of the Graph-GaussianAssembler over baseline methods.

─────────────────────────────  
Experiment 1: End-to-End Comparison on Standard Text-to-3D Benchmarks

Objective: Demonstrate that the full Graph-GaussianAssembler framework produces 3D assets that are more geometrically faithful, have sharper boundaries, and avoid redundant or “multi-face” artifacts compared to baseline approaches (e.g., GaussianDreamer and DiffAssemble).

Design:
• Prepare a standardized set of text prompts (or use an existing benchmark dataset for text-to-3D asset generation).
• For each text prompt, generate 3D assets using:
  – The baseline method (e.g., GaussianDreamer),
  – A reconstruction variant resembling DiffAssemble, and
  – The full Graph-GaussianAssembler.
• Render images of the resulting 3D assets from consistent viewpoints.
• Quantitatively assess the outputs using several metrics:
  – Edge sharpness and structural fidelity (e.g., using a combination of Chamfer Distance on the point clouds and SSIM/PSNR on rendered images),
  – Redundancy measurements, for instance by computing Gaussian density across spatial regions to assess overlapping/redundant Gaussians,
  – Artifact detection (e.g., counting wrongly placed “faces” or extraneous surface patches via geometric consistency checks).
• Qualitative user studies on perceptual realism may be incorporated, where human raters compare the outputs.
• Implementation can be done in Python using PyTorch for model training and differentiable rendering libraries (e.g., PyTorch3D) for visualization/metric computation.

─────────────────────────────  
Experiment 2: Ablation Study on the Graph-Based Reassembly Module

Objective: Isolate and quantify the contribution of the graph-diffusion module in correcting redundant Gaussians and improving edge sharpness.

Design:
• Create at least two variants of the Graph-GaussianAssembler:
  – The complete method with the graph-based reassembly (full GNN denoising module) and
  – An ablated version wherein the graph module is removed or replaced with a simple aggregation (e.g., independent denoising for each Gaussian).
• For both variants, run the asset generation on a controlled subset of prompts that challenge edge precision and object consistency.
• Measure:
  – The degree of redundancy (by assessing the clustering or overlap of Gaussian nodes),
  – Refinement quality of boundaries (using local gradient measures in the rendered images),
  – Overall geometric consistency (via metrics such as Chamfer Distance between the generated Gaussian set and a ground-truth or high-fidelity surrogate model).
• This ablation study can be implemented in Python using frameworks like PyTorch Geometric to easily switch between the attention-based GNN and simpler per-node operations.

─────────────────────────────  
Experiment 3: Evaluation of Adaptive Time Scheduling in Graph Denoising

Objective: Validate that the learned adaptive time scheduling in the graph denoising phase leads to faster convergence, improved stability, and better handling of scene complexity compared to fixed schedules.

Design:
• Implement two versions of the denoising process:
  – The proposed adaptive time scheduling variant, where the schedule is learned based on scene complexity,
  – A baseline variant using a fixed procedural schedule (as typically used in GaussianDreamer/DiffAssemble).
• For a set of text prompts with varying complexity (from simple to complex geometries), run both variants and record:
  – The convergence speed (e.g., number of iterations needed until a stability metric in the Gaussian parameters is reached),
  – The quality of the result after a fixed computation budget (using similar metrics as above: boundary sharpness, redundancy, geometric consistency),
  – Computational efficiency (e.g., total runtime, memory usage).
• This comparison can be implemented in Python by incorporating dynamic vs. static scheduling policies into the iterative graph denoising loop and logging convergence metrics. Visualization of the denoising trajectories (e.g., changes in Gaussian positions over iterations) can provide additional insights.

─────────────────────────────  
Coding Feasibility:
• Each experiment can be implemented in Python using PyTorch as the primary deep learning framework.
• Graph functionality (e.g., for the GNN module) can come from libraries like PyTorch Geometric or DGL.
• Differentiable rendering libraries (such as PyTorch3D) can be leveraged for 3D asset rendering and metric computation.
• Standard Python libraries (NumPy, SciPy, matplotlib) can assist with data handling and result visualization.

With this plan, you can quantitatively and qualitatively demonstrate that Graph-GaussianAssembler improves over the baselines by reducing redundant Gaussians, sharpening edges and boundaries, and adapting the denoising process to scene complexity—all of which are feasible to code and test in Python.

Objective:

Design:

4. Evaluation Metrics

   - Compute Chamfer Distance between corresponding point clouds converted from Gaussian splatting outputs and reference models (or high-fidelity reconstructions).  
   - Measure rendered image quality using SSIM/PSNR.  
  • Redundancy analysis:  
   - Compute local Gaussian density and measure clustering or overlapping Gaussians.  
  • Artifact detection:

─────────────────────────────  
EXPERIMENT 2: ABLATION STUDY ON THE GRAPH-BASED REASSEMBLY MODULE

Objective:
– Isolate the contribution of the graph-based reassembly (i.e., the GNN denoising module that corrects overlapping and redundant Gaussians) compared to a simpler, non-graph-based aggregation/de-noising variant.

Design:
1. Model Variants  
  • Full Graph-GaussianAssembler with an attention-based GNN module for node (Gaussian) reassembly.  
  • Ablated Variant: Replace the GNN module with a per-node independent denoising function (e.g., a simple MLP applied to each Gaussian independently).

2. Evaluation Procedure  
  • Run the asset generation on a controlled subset of challenging prompts (with complex edges and structural consistency requirements).  
  • Compute the following metrics:  
   - Redundancy degree: Use clustering analysis on the Gaussian centers, quantify overlapping using pairwise distance metrics, or compute density measures.  
   - Boundary refinement: Measure local gradients in the rendered images and compare with ground-truth edges (for example, a Sobel edge detector followed by quantitative evaluation).  
   - Overall geometric consistency: Use Chamfer Distance between Gaussian-generated point clouds and a reference high-fidelity model.

3. Example Implementation Outline:

-------------------------------------------------
import torch_geometric
from torch_geometric.nn import GATConv  # For a generic graph attention layer

# Define a simple GNN module for the full method
class GraphDenoisingModule(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels):
        super(GraphDenoisingModule, self).__init__()
        self.conv1 = GATConv(in_channels, 64)
        self.conv2 = GATConv(64, out_channels)

    def forward(self, x, edge_index):
        # x: node features, edge_index: connectivity in COO format.
        x = torch.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index))
        return self.conv2(x, edge_index)

# Define an ablated per-node denoising module (no graph interactions)
class IndependentDenoisingModule(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, out_channels):
        super(IndependentDenoisingModule, self).__init__()
        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(in_channels, 64),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(64, out_channels)
        )

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.mlp(x)

# Integration in the asset generation pipeline
def generate_asset_variant(prompt: str, use_graph: bool = True):
    if use_graph:
        denoise_module = GraphDenoisingModule(in_channels=16, out_channels=16)
        model = GraphGaussianAssembler(denoise_module=denoise_module)
    else:
        denoise_module = IndependentDenoisingModule(in_channels=16, out_channels=16)
        model = GraphGaussianAssembler(denoise_module=denoise_module)
    asset = model.generate_3d_asset(prompt)
    return asset

# Run on a subset of prompts and measure metrics (reuse metric functions from Experiment 1)
test_prompts = ["complex mechanical part", "intricate sculpture"]
for prompt in test_prompts:
    asset_full = generate_asset_variant(prompt, use_graph=True)
    asset_simple = generate_asset_variant(prompt, use_graph=False)
    # Convert to point clouds
    pc_full = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_full)
    pc_simple = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_simple)
    redundancy_metric_full = compute_redundancy_metric(pc_full)
    redundancy_metric_simple = compute_redundancy_metric(pc_simple)
    print(f"Prompt: {prompt}")
    print(f"Redundancy (Graph Module): {redundancy_metric_full}, (Simple Module): {redundancy_metric_simple}")
-------------------------------------------------

Notes:
• The function compute_redundancy_metric could be implemented based on clustering or density estimation (e.g., using simple-knn or scikit-learn clustering).
• By comparing outputs, we can quantitatively assess how much the graph module reduces redundant Gaussians and improves boundary quality.

─────────────────────────────  
EXPERIMENT 3: EVALUATION OF ADAPTIVE TIME SCHEDULING IN GRAPH DENOISING

Objective:
– Validate that the learned adaptive time scheduling during the denoising phase improves convergence speed, stability, and overall quality (e.g., better handling of scene complexity) compared to a fixed/invariant scheduling approach.

Design:
1. Implementation Variants  
  • Adaptive Scheduling: Integrate a learnable module that adjusts the denoising time steps based on scene complexity metrics (for instance, complexity measured as variance or entropy of the Gaussian distributions).  
  • Fixed Scheduling: Use a standard fixed schedule, as implemented in GaussianDreamer or DiffAssemble.

2. Measurement Criteria  
  • Convergence Speed:  
   - Record the number of iterations until the change in Gaussian parameters falls below a threshold.  
  • Final Quality After Fixed Budget:  
   - Use boundary sharpness (local gradient metrics), redundancy measures, and geometric consistency (Chamfer Distance) for evaluation.  
  • Computational Efficiency:  
   - Log runtime per iteration and memory consumption.
   - Visualize the evolution of Gaussian parameters over iterations (a “denoising trajectory plot”).

3. Example Code Outline:

-------------------------------------------------
# Example of an adaptive time scheduler module
class AdaptiveTimeScheduler(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, init_steps=50):
        super(AdaptiveTimeScheduler, self).__init__()
        # A small network that predicts adjustment based on scene complexity.
        self.fc = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(1, 10),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(10, 1)
        )
        self.init_steps = init_steps

    def forward(self, complexity_measure):
        # complexity_measure could be the current variance of the Gaussian parameters.
        adjustment = self.fc(complexity_measure.unsqueeze(0))
        # Dynamic schedule: e.g., modify the number of iterations.
        adaptive_steps = self.init_steps - int(adjustment.item())
        return max(10, adaptive_steps)  # Ensure a minimum of iterations

def denoising_process(model, asset, adaptive_schedule: bool = True):
    # asset contains initial Gaussian parameters among other info.
    num_iterations_fixed = 50  # For fixed schedule
    current_asset = asset
    iteration = 0
    while True:
        # Compute a complexity measure (e.g., variance of Gaussian positions)
        gauss_positions = current_asset['positions']
        complexity_measure = torch.var(gauss_positions)
        # Decide on the number of iterations for the next update cycle
        if adaptive_schedule:
            scheduler = AdaptiveTimeScheduler(init_steps=num_iterations_fixed)
            num_iterations = scheduler(complexity_measure.unsqueeze(0))
        else:
            num_iterations = num_iterations_fixed
        # Perform the denoising update num_iterations times
        for _ in range(num_iterations):
            current_asset = model.denoise_step(current_asset)
            iteration += 1
        # A simple convergence check (e.g., change below a threshold)
        change = torch.norm(current_asset['positions'] - asset['positions'])
        asset = current_asset
        if change < 1e-3 or iteration > 200:  # Stop condition can be refined.
            break
    return current_asset, iteration

# Run experiments on prompts with varying scene complexity.
prompts = ["simple bowl", "busy marketplace", "dense forest scene"]
for prompt in prompts:
    # Initialize the model and asset generation (e.g., using GraphGaussianAssembler)
    model = GraphGaussianAssembler()
    asset_initial = model.initialize_asset(prompt)
    
    # Run with adaptive scheduling
    asset_adaptive, iters_adaptive = denoising_process(model, asset_initial, adaptive_schedule=True)
    # Run with fixed schedule
    asset_fixed, iters_fixed = denoising_process(model, asset_initial, adaptive_schedule=False)
    
    print(f"Prompt: {prompt}")
    print(f"Adaptive Scheduler: iterations = {iters_adaptive}")
    print(f"Fixed Scheduler: iterations = {iters_fixed}")
    
    # Evaluate the final asset quality using previously defined metrics.
    pc_adaptive = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_adaptive)
    pc_fixed = convert_gaussians_to_pointcloud(asset_fixed)
    chamfer_adaptive = compute_chamfer_distance(pc_adaptive, pc_adaptive)  # Replace with reference if available.
    chamfer_fixed = compute_chamfer_distance(pc_fixed, pc_fixed)
    print(f"Chamfer distances -> Adaptive: {chamfer_adaptive:.3f}, Fixed: {chamfer_fixed:.3f}")
-------------------------------------------------

Notes:
• In practice, the adaptive scheduler’s complexity measure might be computed over multiple statistics of the asset (positions, orientations, etc.).
• Visual logs (e.g., plotting Gaussian positions over iterations) can be generated using matplotlib to provide additional insight.

─────────────────────────────  
OVERALL EXPERIMENTAL CONSIDERATIONS

• We use PyTorch as the deep learning backbone and leverage differentiable rendering (PyTorch3D) and graph processing (PyTorch Geometric) to adhere closely to the original implementation guidelines.  
• Standard python libraries (NumPy, SciPy, matplotlib) support dataset handling, clustering, and result visualization.  
• Each experiment is designed to be modular so that experiments 1, 2, and 3 do not overlap unnecessarily but also share common metric computations where appropriate.  
• For reproducibility, configurations will be saved (e.g., yaml files) and random seeds will be set across libraries.
• The experiments can be extended with qualitative user studies by collecting rendered outputs and surveying human raters on perceived realism and artifact presence.

By following these detailed experimental designs and example implementations in PyTorch, we can thoroughly evaluate and verify that the Graph-GaussianAssembler outperforms baseline methods in terms of geometric fidelity, edge sharpness, artifact reduction, and adaptive denoising efficiency.

"""

Additional packages (if available/needed):

import os
import time
import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# PyTorch3D imports for rendering support
from pytorch3d.renderer import (
    FoVPerspectiveCameras,
    RasterizationSettings,
    MeshRenderer,
    MeshRasterizer,
    SoftPhongShader
)
